findings.title: Connection to Suspicious Port: RDP (3389)
findings.description: Connection from 10.128.1.13 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP). This port is commonly associated with attacks or services that should be monitored.

metadata.technical_summary: A connection from 10.128.1.13 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP) indicates suspicious network activity that may be associated with attacks or unauthorized access.

best_control.control_id: 2.3.10.4 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is set to 'Disabled' (Automated)
best_control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous
best_control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Disabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users

control.control_id: 2.3.10.4 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is set to 'Disabled' (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Disabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users

control.control_id: 2.3.10.3 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is set to 'Enabled' (MS only) (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Enabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.

control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Block (default): Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections
Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc or GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, significantly reducing the permissions available to unauthenticated connections. This mitigates potential information disclosure and unauthorized access attempts, which can be precursors to brute force (T1110) or RDP hijacking (T1563.002) attacks. (Source: 2.3.10.4)

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc or GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' and set it to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents anonymous users from listing user accounts and shared folders. This is crucial for preventing attackers from gathering valid usernames for brute-force attacks (T1110) against services like RDP, thereby enhancing the security of your system. (Source: 2.3.10.3)

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc or GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the LocalSystem account from using NULL sessions for authentication. This reduces the risk of information disclosure and unauthorized access via NULL sessions, which could be exploited in conjunction with RDP attacks. (Source: 2.3.11.2)

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Inbound connections to Block by default for Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc or GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab and ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound connections to 'Block' for the Domain Profile ensures that only explicitly allowed traffic can reach the server. While RDP might be allowed via a specific rule, this general hardening measure prevents other unsolicited connections that could be used for reconnaissance or attack, contributing to overall network security. (Source: 9.1.2)

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (gpedit.msc).
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy settings, force a policy update by running 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated command prompt. Then, verify each setting: 1. For 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users': Check the UI path or confirm HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\EveryoneIncludesAnonymous is set to '0' (Disabled). 2. For 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares': Check the UI path or confirm HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\RestrictAnonymous is set to '1' (Enabled). 3. For 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback': Check the UI path to confirm it is set to 'Disabled'. 4. For Windows Firewall Domain Profile Inbound connections: Check the UI path or confirm HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile\DefaultInboundAction is set to '1' (Block).

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

steps.title: Deny RDP logon for Guest accounts
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\User Rights Assignment\Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services. Add 'Guests' to this policy.
steps.explanation: This prevents unauthenticated or guest accounts from logging in via RDP, directly mitigating unauthorized remote access (MITRE ATT&CK T1021.001, T1563.002) and potential brute force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) if guest accounts are targeted. This helps prevent attackers from gaining initial access or escalating privileges.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone' for network permissions. This reduces the attack surface for various network-based attacks, including those that might leverage RDP (MITRE ATT&CK T1021.001, T1563.002) by limiting anonymous access to shared resources and services, thereby hindering initial access (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) or privilege escalation.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the system from reverting to a NULL session if a LocalSystem account cannot authenticate. This hardens the system against potential information disclosure or unauthorized access (MITRE ATT&CK T1021.001, T1563.002) that could be exploited by attackers to enumerate users or shares, which is often a precursor to brute force (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) or other remote access techniques.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server(s) or Group Policy Management Console.
Understanding of Group Policy application and propagation.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. For Step 1: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\User Rights Assignment\Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services' in Group Policy Editor and confirm 'Guests' is listed. 2. For Step 2: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' in Group Policy Editor and confirm it is set to 'Disabled'. You can also check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous is set to 0. 3. For Step 3: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' in Group Policy Editor and confirm it is set to 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows system (Windows Server or Windows 11), open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc) if applying domain-wide. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, which significantly reduces the information and access an unauthenticated attacker can gain. This mitigates potential brute force or password guessing attempts (T1110, T1110.001) by limiting initial reconnaissance capabilities. (Source: 2.3.10.4 (L1))

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server, open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc) if applying domain-wide. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' and set it to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents anonymous users from listing user accounts and shared resources from the Security Account Manager (SAM) database. This is a critical step to prevent attackers from gathering valid usernames (T1110.001) before attempting to brute force RDP credentials. (Source: 2.3.10.3 (L1))

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server, open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc) if applying domain-wide. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the system from reverting to a NULL session for authentication, which could be exploited by attackers to gain unauthorized access or information. This enhances the overall security posture against various reconnaissance and access attempts. (Source: 2.3.11.2)

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 system.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if applying changes at the domain level.
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the Group Policy settings, force a policy update by running 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated command prompt. Then, verify the settings by navigating to the respective UI paths in the Local Group Policy Editor or by checking the registry keys:
1. For 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users': Check HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous. A value of '0' indicates Disabled. (Source: 2.3.10.4 (L1))
2. For 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares': Check HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous. A value of '1' indicates Enabled. (Source: 2.3.10.3 (L1))
3. For 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback': Verify the setting is 'Disabled' in the Local Group Policy Editor. (Source: 2.3.11.2)
Additionally, ensure continuous system monitoring (NIST-SI-04) is in place to detect any further unauthorized RDP connection attempts.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. steps.title: Investigate the observed RDP connection
steps.command_or_action: Review system logs (e.g., Security Event Log, RDP operational logs) on 192.168.220.132 for connection attempts from 10.128.1.176 around the time of the finding. Check for successful logins, failed login attempts, and associated user accounts. If the connection was unauthorized, initiate incident response procedures.
steps.explanation: This step aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to 'Analyze detected events and anomalies' and 'Identify unauthorized use of the system'. Understanding the nature of the connection (successful/failed, legitimate/malicious) is critical for appropriate response.

steps.title: Ensure Windows Firewall blocks inbound connections on Public profile
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security - Local Group Policy Object\Windows Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Public Profile' tab and ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, use the command line: netsh advfirewall set publicprofile state on; netsh advfirewall set publicprofile inboundaction block
steps.explanation: This configuration (9.3.2) prevents unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP, from public networks, significantly reducing the attack surface for remote access services. This helps mitigate T1110 (Brute Force) and T1563.002 (RDP Hijacking) by limiting exposure.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Set the following Group Policy UI path to 'Disabled': Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting (2.3.10.4) prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, which can limit their ability to enumerate system information or access resources, thereby reducing the risk of reconnaissance and initial access attempts.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'. This can be found in Group Policy under: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback (2.3.11.2) prevents the LocalSystem account from falling back to a NULL session when authenticating to network resources. This enhances security by preventing potential information disclosure or unauthorized access that could be exploited by attackers.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (if applying via GPO).
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. For Step 1, confirm review of logs and any incident response actions taken. 2. For Step 2, navigate to 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' and verify that for the 'Public Profile', 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. You can also run 'netsh advfirewall show publicprofile' and check 'Inbound Action'. 3. For Step 3, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and confirm 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is 'Disabled'. Alternatively, check the registry at HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa and ensure 'EveryoneIncludesAnonymous' is set to '0'. 4. For Step 4, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections for the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This setting prevents all unsolicited inbound connections, including RDP, to systems within the domain, significantly reducing the attack surface and mitigating brute force attempts (MITRE T1110, T1110.001) against RDP services. This aligns with the principle of least privilege for network access and helps detect and prevent unauthorized connections as per NIST-SI-04.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (if applying via GPO).
remediation.verification_procedure: On the affected Windows system, open a command prompt as administrator and run: reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile /v DefaultInboundAction. Confirm the output shows DefaultInboundAction REG_DWORD 0x1, which corresponds to 'Block (default)'. Additionally, attempt an RDP connection from the source IP (10.128.1.176) to the target system (10.100.104.132) and verify that the connection is blocked, confirming the firewall rule is active. This ensures the system is monitored to detect and prevent unauthorized network connections as specified in NIST-SI-04.

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default for the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: On a Domain Controller or a system with Group Policy Management Tools installed, open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Apply this GPO to the relevant Organizational Units containing the Windows Server and Windows 11 machines.
steps.explanation: This action implements a default deny policy for inbound connections on the domain profile, significantly reducing the attack surface by preventing unauthorized RDP connections and other services. This directly addresses the potential for brute force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) by blocking unsolicited access.

steps.title: Create Specific Inbound Firewall Rules for Authorized RDP Access (If Required)
steps.command_or_action: If RDP access is legitimately required for administrative purposes, create a new inbound rule in the Windows Firewall with Advanced Security GPO (or locally if not centrally managed). Navigate to 'Inbound Rules', right-click and select 'New Rule...'. Configure the rule to 'Allow the connection' for 'Port' 3389 (RDP) and specify 'Remote IP address' to 'These IP addresses' (list authorized source IPs) or 'Users' (list authorized user groups). Ensure this rule is applied only to necessary systems.
steps.explanation: After blocking all inbound connections by default, this step allows for controlled and authorized RDP access. By restricting RDP to specific source IP addresses or user groups, the risk of unauthorized access and brute force attempts is minimized, while maintaining necessary administrative functionality.

steps.title: Review and Enhance System Monitoring for RDP Activity
steps.command_or_action: Review existing security information and event management (SIEM) or logging systems for RDP connection logs. Ensure that alerts are configured for: 1. Multiple failed RDP login attempts from a single source IP. 2. RDP connections from unusual or unauthorized source IP addresses. 3. Successful RDP logins by accounts outside of normal administrative hours. Adjust monitoring thresholds and alert severities as needed.
steps.explanation: This step aligns with NIST-SI-04 by enhancing system monitoring to detect and respond to potential attacks and unauthorized use. Proactive monitoring for suspicious RDP activity helps identify and mitigate brute force attempts and unauthorized access, providing early warning of potential compromises.

prerequisites: Administrative access to a Domain Controller or a system with Group Policy Management Tools for Windows Server/Windows 11.
Access to security monitoring tools and logs (e.g., SIEM, Windows Event Viewer).
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. On a target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' and confirm that for the 'Domain Profile', 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block'. 2. Attempt to establish an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address; confirm the connection is blocked. 3. Attempt to establish an RDP connection from an authorized IP address (if a specific rule was created); confirm the connection is successful. 4. Review security logs (e.g., Windows Event Viewer Security logs, SIEM) to confirm that RDP connection attempts (both blocked and allowed) are being logged and that any configured alerts for suspicious RDP activity are functioning as expected.

steps.title: Review RDP Connection Logs for Observed Activity
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following commands to review RDP connection events:
Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational" | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 21 -or $_.Id -eq 24 -or $_.Id -eq 25} | Format-List -Property TimeCreated, Id, LevelDisplayName, Message

Also, open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc), navigate to 'Windows Logs' -> 'Security', and filter for Event IDs 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) with Logon Type 10 (RemoteInteractive) to identify the source IP (10.128.1.176) and user associated with the observed connection.
steps.explanation: This step directly investigates the observed RDP connection to determine its legitimacy, the user involved, and the exact timing. Analyzing these logs is crucial for identifying if the connection was authorized or an indicator of brute force attempts (T1110) or potential session hijacking (T1563.002), aligning with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring.

steps.title: Configure Advanced Audit Policy for RDP Logon/Logoff
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open an elevated Command Prompt or PowerShell and run:
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Logon" /success:enable /failure:enable
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Other Logon/Logoff Events" /success:enable /failure:enable

Alternatively, open `gpedit.msc` (Local Group Policy Editor), navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Advanced Audit Policy Configuration' -> 'Audit Policies' -> 'Logon/Logoff'. Enable 'Audit Logon' for Success and Failure, and 'Audit Other Logon/Logoff Events' for Success and Failure.
steps.explanation: This ensures that all RDP logon and logoff attempts, both successful and failed, are comprehensively recorded in the Security Event Log (Event IDs 4624, 4625). This detailed logging is critical for detecting unauthorized access, brute force attacks (T1110), and monitoring session activity for potential hijacking (T1563.002), thereby enhancing system monitoring as per NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Configure SIEM/Monitoring Alerts for RDP Anomalies
steps.command_or_action:
Within your organization's SIEM or monitoring solution (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel), create or verify existing alerts for the following conditions on the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132):
1.  Multiple failed RDP logon attempts from a single source IP within a short timeframe (e.g., 5 failures in 5 minutes), indicative of brute force (T1110).
2.  Successful RDP logons from unusual geographic locations or unexpected source IPs.
3.  RDP sessions with unusually long durations or frequent disconnections/reconnections, which could indicate session hijacking (T1563.002).
4.  RDP logons outside of normal business hours for critical accounts.
5.  Alert on Event IDs 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) with Logon Type 10 (RemoteInteractive) from unexpected source IPs.
steps.explanation: Proactive alerting on RDP anomalies is a core component of system monitoring (NIST-SI-04). This helps detect and respond quickly to brute force attacks (T1110) and potential RDP session hijacking attempts (T1563.002) by flagging suspicious login patterns or session behaviors.

steps.title: Harden Windows Firewall for RDP Access
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132):
1.  Open `wf.msc` (Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security).
2.  Navigate to 'Inbound Rules'.
3.  Locate the 'Remote Desktop (TCP-In)' rule (or similar rules for port 3389).
4.  Right-click and select 'Properties'.
5.  On the 'Scope' tab, under 'Remote IP address', select 'These IP addresses' and add only the specific IP addresses or subnets that are authorized to initiate RDP connections to 192.168.220.132 (e.g., 10.128.1.176 if it's authorized, or a management subnet).
6.  To ensure the 'Public' profile blocks inbound connections, navigate to `wf.msc` -> 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties' (in the central pane) -> 'Public Profile' tab. Ensure 'State' for 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)' as per CIS 9.3.2.
steps.explanation: Restricting RDP access at the host firewall level minimizes the attack surface and prevents unauthorized brute force attempts (T1110) by limiting who can even attempt to connect. This aligns with CIS 9.3.2 and general network security best practices.

steps.title: Configure Palo Alto Firewall for RDP Monitoring and Restriction
steps.command_or_action:
Access the Palo Alto Firewall management interface:
1.  **Review Security Policies:** Identify any security policies allowing RDP (TCP/3389) traffic from 10.128.1.176 to 192.168.220.132. Ensure these policies are strictly necessary and source/destination IPs are narrowed down to only authorized entities.
2.  **Enable Logging:** For any RDP-related security policies, ensure logging is enabled for both session start and end.
3.  **Threat Prevention Profiles:** Apply appropriate Threat Prevention profiles (e.g., Vulnerability Protection, Anti-Spyware) to RDP traffic to detect and block known RDP exploits or brute force attempts.
4.  **User-ID Integration:** If User-ID is deployed, ensure it's leveraged to identify the actual user initiating RDP connections, enhancing visibility for monitoring (NIST-SI-04).
5.  **Alerting:** Configure the Palo Alto Firewall to generate alerts for excessive failed RDP connection attempts or unusual RDP traffic patterns.
steps.explanation: Leveraging the Palo Alto Firewall for RDP traffic inspection and logging provides an additional layer of defense and monitoring (NIST-SI-04). It can detect and prevent brute force attacks (T1110) and provide crucial network-level visibility for investigating potential session hijacking (T1563.002).

steps.title: Set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132):
1.  Open `gpedit.msc` (Local Group Policy Editor).
2.  Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Administrative Templates' -> 'Network' -> 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'.
3.  Double-click on 'NetBT NodeType configuration'.
4.  Set the policy to 'Enabled'.
5.  From the 'Options:' dropdown, select 'P-node (recommended)'.
6.  Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
7.  Run `gpupdate /force` in an elevated command prompt to apply the changes.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to P-node (Peer-to-Peer) minimizes NetBIOS broadcast traffic, which can reduce the attack surface for NetBIOS-related enumeration and attacks. While not directly preventing RDP, it contributes to overall system hardening and reduces information leakage that could aid attackers in reconnaissance (T1110). This aligns with control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132):
1.  Open `gpedit.msc` (Local Group Policy Editor).
2.  Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options'.
3.  Double-click on 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
4.  Set the policy to 'Disabled'.
5.  Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
6.  Run `gpupdate /force` in an elevated command prompt to apply the changes.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions, which can be exploited for information gathering or privilege escalation. This is a general security hardening measure that reduces the overall attack surface and helps prevent various reconnaissance and access techniques (T1110). This aligns with control 2.3.11.2.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server (192.168.220.132).
Access to the organization's SIEM or monitoring solution.
Administrative access to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface.
Understanding of network topology to identify if the Palo Alto Firewall is in the path of the observed RDP connection.
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying Group Policy changes, verify the settings by checking the relevant registry keys:
*   For NetBT NodeType: `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType` should be `0x2` (P-node).
*   For NULL session fallback: `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\AllowLsaNullSessionFallback` should be `0`.
Confirm Windows Firewall rules for RDP are restricted to authorized source IPs and the Public profile's inbound connections are blocked.
Verify that RDP logon/logoff events (Event IDs 4624, 4625, and TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager events) are being logged in the Windows Event Viewer.
Confirm that alerts for RDP anomalies are configured and tested in the SIEM/monitoring solution.
Verify Palo Alto Firewall policies are updated and logging RDP traffic as expected.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections for the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, or via Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) for domain-joined systems, navigate to:
Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile
Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default when the system is connected to a domain network. This is a critical step to prevent unauthorized access attempts, including RDP connections, and mitigates the risk of brute force attacks (T1110) by ensuring only explicitly allowed traffic can reach the system.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, or Group Policy Administrator privileges for domain-wide application.
remediation.verification_procedure: To verify the setting, navigate to the UI path specified in Step 1 and confirm 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, check the registry key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile and confirm the value of 'DefaultInboundAction' is set to '1' (which corresponds to Block). Attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized source (e.g., 10.128.1.164) to the target machine (10.100.105.132) and confirm it fails.

steps.title: Investigate RDP Connection Logs on Target Server
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute the following commands to review RDP connection events and security logs around the time of the observed connection:

Get-WinEvent -LogName "Security" | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 4624 -or $_.Id -eq 4625 -or $_.Id -eq 4648} | Format-List -Property TimeCreated, Id, Message

Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational" | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 21 -or $_.Id -eq 24 -or $_.Id -eq 25} | Format-List -Property TimeCreated, Id, Message

Look for successful (ID 4624) or failed (ID 4625) login attempts, explicit credential usage (ID 4648), and RDP session connect/disconnect events (IDs 21, 24, 25) originating from 10.128.1.164.
steps.explanation: This step directly addresses NIST-SI-04 by monitoring system events to detect unauthorized connections and potential attacks (T1110, T1563.002). Reviewing logs helps determine if the connection was legitimate, who initiated it, and if any suspicious activity occurred during the session.

steps.title: Review Network Firewall Logs for Connection Details
steps.command_or_action: Access the Palo Alto Firewall management interface. Navigate to Monitor > Logs > Traffic. Filter logs for source IP 10.128.1.164, destination IP 192.168.220.132, and destination port 3389 (RDP) around the time the connection was observed. Check if the connection was allowed or denied, and review any associated threat logs.
steps.explanation: This step aligns with NIST-SI-04 by monitoring external and internal network traffic. Analyzing firewall logs provides crucial context about the network path, security policy enforcement, and any potential threat detections related to the suspicious RDP connection.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute:

Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" -Name "NullSessionFallback" -Value 0 -Force

Alternatively, configure via Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options > 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' prevents LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session if it cannot authenticate using the computer's credentials. This hardens the system against potential privilege escalation or information disclosure vulnerabilities, aligning with general security best practices and control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Configure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to P-node
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute:

Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" -Name "NodeType" -Value 2 -Force

Alternatively, configure via Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP > 'NetBT NodeType configuration' and set it to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (Peer-to-Peer) configures the system to rely primarily on a WINS server for name resolution, reducing reliance on broadcast-based name resolution. This can enhance network security by limiting exposure to certain types of NetBIOS-related attacks, addressing control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Ensure Windows Firewall Inbound Connections are Blocked by Default
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132) or Windows 11 client, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute:

Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Domain -DefaultInboundAction Block
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -DefaultInboundAction Block

Verify that specific inbound rules for RDP (port 3389) are configured to allow connections only from trusted source IPs or networks, if RDP access is required.
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound action to 'Block' for Domain and Public profiles significantly reduces the attack surface by preventing unsolicited incoming connections. This aligns with controls 9.1.2 and 9.3.2, ensuring that only explicitly allowed services, like RDP from authorized sources, can establish connections.

steps.title: Restrict RDP Access to Authorized Users and Groups
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open 'Computer Management' (compmgmt.msc), navigate to 'Local Users and Groups' > 'Groups', and select 'Remote Desktop Users'. Ensure that only explicitly authorized user accounts or groups are members. Remove any unauthorized users.

To list current members via PowerShell: `Get-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users"`
To remove a user: `Remove-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users" -Member "<username>"`
steps.explanation: Limiting RDP access to only necessary and authorized personnel reduces the risk of unauthorized access and brute-force attacks (T1110). This is a critical step in securing remote access services and aligns with the monitoring objectives of NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Block Unauthorized Source IP at Network Firewall (If Confirmed Malicious)
steps.command_or_action:
If the RDP connection from 10.128.1.164 is confirmed to be unauthorized or malicious after investigation, access the Palo Alto Firewall management interface. Navigate to Policies > Security. Create a new security policy with:

Source Zone: (Zone where 10.128.1.164 resides)
Source Address: 10.128.1.164
Destination Zone: (Zone where 192.168.220.132 resides)
Destination Address: 192.168.220.132
Application: rdp (or service-tcp-3389)
Action: Deny

Position this rule above any existing allow rules that might permit this traffic.
steps.explanation: Proactively blocking confirmed unauthorized or malicious source IPs at the network perimeter (Palo Alto Firewall) prevents future connection attempts and mitigates ongoing threats. This is a direct response to detecting an unauthorized connection, as per NIST-SI-04, and helps prevent brute-force or session hijacking attempts.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132)
Access to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface
PowerShell access with administrative privileges on Windows systems
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the remediation steps:
1. Re-check Windows Security and TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager logs on 192.168.220.132 to confirm no further unauthorized RDP connections from 10.128.1.164 are observed.
2. Verify the 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' setting is 'Disabled' by checking the registry key HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\NullSessionFallback (should be 0) or Group Policy.
3. Verify the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' is set to 'P-node' by checking the registry key HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType (should be 2) or Group Policy.
4. Confirm Windows Firewall profiles for Domain and Public have 'DefaultInboundAction' set to 'Block' using `Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select-Object Name, DefaultInboundAction`.
5. Verify that only authorized users are members of the 'Remote Desktop Users' group.
6. If a block rule was implemented on the Palo Alto Firewall, attempt a connection from 10.128.1.164 (if safe and in a controlled test environment) to confirm it is denied.

steps.title: Block Inbound Connections on Windows Firewall (Domain Profile)
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy, navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile and set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action directly prevents unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP, to systems within the domain. By blocking these connections, you significantly reduce the attack surface for brute force attempts (T1110) against RDP services, making it harder for attackers to guess credentials.

steps.title: Block Inbound Connections on Windows Firewall (Public Profile)
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy, navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile and set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This step extends protection to systems operating on public or untrusted networks, preventing unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP. This further mitigates the risk of brute force attacks (T1110) by ensuring that RDP services are not exposed to untrusted environments.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Set the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType to 0x2 (P-node). This can be deployed via Group Policy or manually.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node (Peer-to-peer) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution relies solely on directed queries to a WINS server, rather than broadcasts. This reduces network noise and the potential for attackers to perform reconnaissance (a precursor to T1110 brute force) by intercepting broadcast traffic or spoofing NetBIOS names.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy, navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options and set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents unauthenticated users from accessing certain system information. Attackers often use NULL sessions for reconnaissance to gather user accounts, share names, and other details that can be leveraged in brute force attacks (T1110). Disabling this setting removes a potential avenue for such information gathering.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Group Policy Management Editor or target Windows Servers/Workstations.
Understanding of Group Policy deployment.
remediation.verification_procedure: For Firewall settings: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties in Group Policy and confirm that both Domain Profile\Inbound connections and Public Profile\Inbound connections are set to Block (default). Alternatively, check the registry at HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction and HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\PublicProfile:DefaultInboundAction to ensure they are set to 1 (Block). For NetBT NodeType: Verify the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType is set to 0x2. For NULL Session Fallback: Verify the Group Policy Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback is set to Disabled.

control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Block (default): Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections

steps.title: Disable anonymous users from having 'Everyone' permissions
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: This prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone', significantly reducing the scope of permissions an unauthenticated attacker might gain, thereby mitigating unauthorized access attempts (T1021).

steps.title: Prevent anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares and set it to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: This setting prevents unauthenticated attackers from listing user accounts and shared folders, which is a common reconnaissance technique used before brute-force or password guessing attacks (T1110, T1110.001).

steps.title: Disable NULL session fallback for LocalSystem
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this prevents the system from falling back to a NULL session when a LocalSystem account attempts to access a network resource, reducing the risk of unauthorized access or information disclosure (T1021).

steps.title: Set Windows Firewall Inbound connections to Block by default
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections and set it to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This hardens the firewall by blocking all inbound connections by default on the Domain Profile, preventing unauthorized network access. Ensure specific rules are in place for legitimate services like RDP if needed (T1021).

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (if applying via GPO) or Local Security Policy Editor.
remediation.verification_procedure:
To verify the remediation, perform the following checks:
1. Confirm 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is 'Disabled' by navigating to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options or by checking the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous is '0'.
2. Confirm 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is 'Enabled' by navigating to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options or by checking the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous is '1'.
3. Confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled' by navigating to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.
4. Confirm 'Inbound connections' for the Domain Profile in Windows Firewall with Advanced Security is 'Block (default)' by navigating to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile or by checking the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction is '1'.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

steps.title: Block the suspicious source IP at the network firewall
steps.command_or_action: Log in to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface. Navigate to Policies > Security. Create a new security rule with the following parameters: Source Zone: [Appropriate External/Untrusted Zone], Source Address: 10.128.1.176, Destination Zone: [Appropriate Internal/DMZ Zone], Destination Address: 10.100.104.132, Application: rdp (or port 3389), Action: Deny. Ensure the rule is placed above any existing RDP allow rules.
steps.explanation: Blocking the observed suspicious source IP at the network perimeter prevents further unauthorized RDP connection attempts to the target system, directly addressing the potential for attacks and unauthorized network connections as per NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default
steps.command_or_action: For Windows Server and Windows 11 systems, open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab and set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Apply this policy to relevant OUs containing the affected systems.
steps.explanation: Setting the Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface by preventing unsolicited connections, including RDP, unless explicitly allowed by a specific rule. This aligns with control 9.1.2 to prevent unauthorized remote connections.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' on Windows Servers
steps.command_or_action: For Windows Server systems, open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'. Apply this policy to relevant OUs containing Windows Servers.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing system information, which can be exploited during reconnaissance phases of an attack. This is a general hardening measure for Windows Server, as per control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Enable and monitor RDP connection logging
steps.command_or_action: Ensure that RDP connection attempts are logged on Windows Server and Windows 11 systems. This can be configured via Group Policy: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Advanced Audit Policy Configuration\Audit Policies\Logon/Logoff. Enable 'Audit Credential Validation' for Success and Failure. Also, ensure 'Audit Other Logon/Logoff Events' is enabled. Confirm that these logs are collected by the organization's SIEM or log management solution for analysis.
steps.explanation: Enhanced logging and monitoring of RDP connection attempts are critical for detecting and analyzing suspicious activity, unauthorized access, and potential brute-force attacks (T1110) or RDP hijacking (T1563.002). This directly supports the system monitoring objectives outlined in NIST-SI-04.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Palo Alto Firewall management interface.
Administrative access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) for Windows Server and Windows 11.
Understanding of organizational network zones and RDP access requirements.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt an RDP connection from the blocked IP (10.128.1.176) to the target (10.100.104.132) and confirm it is denied by the Palo Alto Firewall. 2. On a Windows Server or Windows 11 system affected by the GPO, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' and verify that the 'Domain Profile' shows 'Inbound connections: Blocked'. 3. On a Windows Server, open 'Local Security Policy' (or check effective GPO settings) and confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'. 4. Check the Security Event Log (Event Viewer) on a target Windows system for Event ID 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) related to RDP connections, confirming that RDP attempts are being logged.

steps.title: Disable anonymous 'Everyone' permissions
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' in Group Policy Editor and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from inheriting 'Everyone' permissions, which significantly reduces the attack surface for unauthorized access attempts, including those potentially leveraging RDP. This helps mitigate brute force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Prevent anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' in Group Policy Editor and set it to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents unauthenticated users from listing user accounts and shared folders. This makes it harder for attackers to gather information (e.g., valid usernames) that could be used in brute-force or password-guessing attacks (T1110.001) against services like RDP.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' in Group Policy Editor and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions, further reducing the attack surface and potential for information disclosure or unauthorized access, which could be exploited in conjunction with remote services (T1021).

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections' in Group Policy Editor and set it to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound connection behavior to 'Block' for the Domain Profile enhances network security by preventing unsolicited incoming connections, including those to RDP, unless explicitly allowed by a specific rule. This reduces the overall attack surface and helps prevent unauthorized access (T1021).

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine(s).
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (for domain-wide changes) or Local Security Policy Editor (for local machine changes).
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the Group Policy or local security settings, perform the following checks:
1.  Verify that the registry value 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous' is set to '0'.
2.  Verify that the registry value 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous' is set to '1'.
3.  Confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled' by navigating to the UI path in Local Security Policy or Group Policy Editor.
4.  Verify that the registry value 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction' is set to '1'.
