control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Block (default): Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections
Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: Open 'gpedit.msc' (Local Group Policy Editor). Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options'. Locate and double-click 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'. Set the policy to 'Disabled'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents unauthenticated users from accessing certain system resources, which can be exploited during reconnaissance or privilege escalation. This reduces the attack surface and helps mitigate brute force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) by limiting information available to potential attackers.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to block inbound connections
steps.command_or_action: Open 'gpedit.msc' (Local Group Policy Editor). Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Policies' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Windows Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab. Under 'State', ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Domain Profile prevents unauthorized network access, including RDP (port 3389). This is a critical step to prevent brute force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized remote access attempts against the server.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to block inbound connections
steps.command_or_action: Open 'gpedit.msc' (Local Group Policy Editor). Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Policies' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Windows Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Public Profile' tab. Under 'State', ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Public Profile is essential for servers exposed to untrusted networks. This prevents unauthorized RDP connections (port 3389) and significantly mitigates brute force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) and other forms of unauthorized remote access.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server.
Understanding of Group Policy management (if applying via GPO).
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the changes, open 'wf.msc' (Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security). Verify that for both 'Domain Profile' and 'Public Profile', the 'Inbound connections' setting is 'Block'. Additionally, attempt to establish an RDP connection from an unauthorized host; it should be blocked. You can also check the registry values: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction and HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\PublicProfile:DefaultInboundAction should both be '1'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound public connections
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -Enabled True -DefaultInboundAction Block
steps.explanation: This action ensures that the Windows Firewall blocks all inbound connections on the Public network profile by default. This is a critical step to prevent unauthorized Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) access attempts (T1110 - Brute Force) from untrusted networks, significantly reducing the attack surface and mitigating potential RDP hijacking (T1563.002) by preventing initial unauthorized access.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" /v "AllowLsaNullSessionFallback" /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing certain system information. This hardening measure reduces the potential for attackers to perform reconnaissance, which could precede brute force attacks (T1110) or help identify targets for RDP hijacking (T1563.002).

steps.title: Configure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'P-node'
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" /v "NodeType" /t REG_DWORD /d 2 /f
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) minimizes broadcast traffic for name resolution. This reduces the potential for network-based reconnaissance that could be used by attackers to identify targets for RDP brute force (T1110) or other network-based attacks.

steps.title: Review RDP connection logs and enhance monitoring for suspicious activity
steps.command_or_action: On the affected Windows Server, open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc) and navigate to 'Windows Logs' -> 'Security'. Filter for Event ID 4624 (Successful logon) and 4625 (Failed logon) with Logon Type 10 (RemoteInteractive). Investigate any suspicious RDP connections, especially from unexpected source IPs (like 10.128.1.176 mentioned in the finding). Configure your SIEM or monitoring solution to alert on multiple failed RDP logon attempts from a single source IP (indicating T1110 - Brute Force) or unusual RDP session durations/activity (potential T1563.002 - RDP Hijacking).
steps.explanation: This step directly addresses the observed RDP connection by investigating its legitimacy and enhancing ongoing monitoring. Proactive monitoring and analysis of RDP logon events are crucial for detecting and responding to unauthorized access attempts, including brute force attacks (T1110) and potential RDP session hijacking (T1563.002), aligning with NIST-SI-04's requirement for system monitoring and analysis of detected events and anomalies.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the affected Windows Server(s)
Access to your organization's SIEM or monitoring solution (if applicable)
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the steps, verify the following:
1.  **Firewall:** Open PowerShell as Administrator and run `Get-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public | Select-Object Name, Enabled, DefaultInboundAction`. Confirm 'DefaultInboundAction' is 'Block'.
2.  **NULL Session Fallback:** Open PowerShell as Administrator and run `Get-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa -Name AllowLsaNullSessionFallback`. Confirm 'AllowLsaNullSessionFallback' is '0'.
3.  **NetBT NodeType:** Open PowerShell as Administrator and run `Get-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters -Name NodeType`. Confirm 'NodeType' is '2'.
4.  **Monitoring:** Review your SIEM or monitoring solution to confirm that alerts for suspicious RDP activity are configured and active. Monitor for any new RDP connections from the identified source IP (10.128.1.176) or other unauthorized sources.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone' for permissions. This reduces the attack surface by limiting information disclosure and access, which can be exploited by attackers for reconnaissance or to facilitate brute-force attacks (T1110.001) against network services like RDP.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the LocalSystem account from using unauthenticated sessions. This mitigates a potential vector for attackers to enumerate system information or gain unauthorized access, which could be leveraged in brute-force attacks (T1110.001) or other forms of unauthorized access.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections for the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections. Set this policy to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the Windows Firewall Domain Profile to block inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface. This directly mitigates suspicious RDP activity by preventing unauthorized connections to port 3389, thereby protecting against brute-force attacks (T1110.001) and other unauthorized access attempts. Specific RDP access should be re-enabled only for authorized sources and IP ranges.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if applying via Group Policy.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy, run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server. Then, verify the settings by navigating to the respective UI paths in the Local Security Policy editor (secpol.msc) or by checking the registry keys: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous (should be 0 for Disabled) and HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction (should be 1 for Block). Attempting an RDP connection from an unauthorized source should fail.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). steps.title: Block Inbound Connections on Public Network Profile
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows machine (192.168.220.132), open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. In the left pane, click 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'. Navigate to the 'Public Profile' tab. Under 'State', ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
steps.explanation: This action ensures that all unsolicited inbound connections, including RDP, are blocked when the system is operating on a public network profile. This significantly reduces the attack surface and helps prevent unauthorized access attempts, aligning with a strong security posture.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows machine (192.168.220.132), open the Local Group Policy Editor by typing 'gpedit.msc' in the Run dialog (Windows Key + R) and pressing Enter. Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options'. Locate and double-click 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'. Set the policy to 'Disabled'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions. This hardens the system against potential information disclosure and unauthorized access, contributing to overall system security.

steps.title: Verify and Enhance RDP Connection Monitoring
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows machine (192.168.220.132), open 'Event Viewer' and navigate to 'Windows Logs' -> 'Security'. Review recent events for RDP-related activity (e.g., Event ID 4624 for successful logins, 4625 for failed logins, 4648 for explicit credential use). Confirm that these security events are being collected and forwarded to your organization's central SIEM or monitoring solution. If not, ensure the appropriate logging and forwarding configurations are in place.
steps.explanation: Continuous monitoring of RDP connection attempts is crucial for detecting brute-force attacks (MITRE T1110) and unauthorized remote access (MITRE T1563.002). Ensuring these events are logged and analyzed allows for timely detection and response to suspicious activity.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows machine (192.168.220.132)
Access to security event logs and SIEM/monitoring solution
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Confirm the 'Public Profile' inbound connections are set to 'Block (default)' in Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security. 2. Verify that 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is set to 'Disabled' in Local Group Policy Editor. 3. Check the SIEM or monitoring solution to confirm that RDP connection events (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4648) from 192.168.220.132 are being received and processed, and that alerts are configured for suspicious RDP activity.

steps.title: Block inbound RDP connections on the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab. Under 'State', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound RDP connections on the firewall prevents unauthorized access attempts to port 3389, directly mitigating the suspicious network activity and reducing the attack surface for remote services (T1021).

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone', limiting their ability to access resources and perform reconnaissance, which can precede brute-force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents anonymous users from listing user accounts and shared folders. This is crucial for mitigating password guessing attacks (T1110.001) by making it harder for attackers to discover valid usernames.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents the system from falling back to a NULL session for LocalSystem, further restricting anonymous access and reducing the risk of privilege escalation or information disclosure.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server or domain controller for Group Policy Management.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy, run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server. Then, verify the settings by navigating to the respective UI paths in the Group Policy Management Editor: 1. Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections should be 'Block (default)'. 2. Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users should be 'Disabled'. 3. Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares should be 'Enabled'. 4. Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback should be 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections for the Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action:
To configure via Group Policy (recommended for domain-joined systems):
1. Open Group Policy Management Editor.
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties.
3. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab.
4. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.

To configure via Local Policy (for standalone systems or immediate local application):
1. Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc).
2. In the left pane, right-click 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security on Local Computer' and select 'Properties'.
3. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab.
4. Under 'State', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action directly implements the recommended security posture by blocking all unsolicited inbound connections, including RDP, on the domain network profile. This significantly reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized access attempts, addressing potential brute force (T1110) and password guessing (T1110.001) tactics.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 system.
Access to Group Policy Management Console if applying via Group Policy.
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. **Confirm Firewall Setting:**
   - Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc).
   - Right-click 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security on Local Computer', select 'Properties'.
   - Navigate to the 'Domain Profile' tab and confirm 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'.
   - Alternatively, verify the registry setting: `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction` should be `1` (Block).
2. **Test RDP Connection:**
   - From the source IP (10.128.1.176), attempt to establish an RDP connection to the target (10.100.104.132). The connection should fail.
3. **Review Firewall Logs (NIST-SI-04):**
   - On the target system, open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc).
   - Navigate to 'Applications and Services Logs' -> 'Microsoft' -> 'Windows' -> 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Firewall'.
   - Filter for Event ID '5152' (The Windows Firewall blocked an application from accepting an incoming connection) or '5157' (The Windows Firewall blocked a connection). Look for entries related to port 3389 and the source IP 10.128.1.176, confirming the firewall is actively blocking the connection attempts.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) or Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc): 1. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties 2. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab. 3. Under 'State', ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. 4. Repeat for 'Private Profile' and 'Public Profile' if applicable to your network configuration, setting 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections by default on the Windows Firewall prevents unauthorized RDP connections from reaching the server, directly mitigating brute-force attacks (T1110) and other unauthorized access attempts. This aligns with control 9.1.2 (L1) from Windows Firewall best practices.

steps.title: Ensure RDP logging is enabled for logon events
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) or Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc): 1. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Advanced Audit Policy Configuration\Audit Policies\Logon/Logoff 2. Double-click 'Audit Logon' and configure for 'Success' and 'Failure'. 3. Double-click 'Audit Other Logon/Logoff Events' and configure for 'Success' and 'Failure'. Alternatively, use the command line (requires elevated privileges): auditpol /set /subcategory:"Logon" /success:enable /failure:enable auditpol /set /subcategory:"Other Logon/Logoff Events" /success:enable /failure:enable
steps.explanation: Enabling comprehensive auditing for logon events ensures that all RDP connection attempts, both successful and failed, are recorded in the Security Event Log. This is crucial for detecting brute-force attacks (T1110) and other suspicious login activities, as mandated by NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring.

steps.title: Monitor Windows Security Event Logs for RDP activity
steps.command_or_action: Regularly review the Windows Security Event Log (eventvwr.msc) for the following Event IDs: - Event ID 4624: An account was successfully logged on. - Event ID 4625: An account failed to log on. Filter by 'Logon Type' 10 (RemoteInteractive) to specifically view RDP logon attempts. Look for a high volume of failed logon attempts from a single source IP or user account, which could indicate a brute-force attack.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring and analysis of RDP logon events are essential for identifying and responding to potential brute-force attacks (T1110) or unauthorized access attempts. This directly implements the 'Analyze detected events and anomalies' requirement of NIST-SI-04, allowing for timely detection and response to suspicious activity.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if managing policies centrally.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt to establish an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address or system. The connection should be blocked by the firewall.2. Verify that successful and failed RDP logon attempts are recorded in the Windows Security Event Log (Event IDs 4624 and 4625) with Logon Type 10 (RemoteInteractive).

steps.title: Investigate the Observed RDP Connection
steps.command_or_action: On the target system (192.168.220.132), open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc). Navigate to "Applications and Services Logs" -> "Microsoft" -> "Windows" -> "TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager" -> "Operational" and "TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager" -> "Operational". Filter for Event IDs 21, 24, 25, 39, 40, 1149, and 261 for the timestamp of the observed connection from 10.128.1.164.
steps.explanation: This step helps determine if the RDP connection from 10.128.1.164 was legitimate, which user account was used, and if it was successfully authenticated. This directly addresses the 'potential suspicious activity' aspect of the finding and aligns with NIST-SI-04 for analyzing detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Review and Restrict RDP Access Permissions
steps.command_or_action: On the target system (192.168.220.132), open "System Properties" -> "Remote" tab. Click "Select Users..." to review which users or groups have permission to connect remotely. Remove any unauthorized users or groups. Alternatively, use PowerShell to check RDP status and group members: `(Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server").fDenyTSConnections` (0=enabled, 1=disabled) and `Get-LocalGroupMember "Remote Desktop Users"`.
steps.explanation: Limiting RDP access to only authorized personnel reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized remote access, aligning with NIST-SI-04's objective of detecting unauthorized connections.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpmc.msc). Navigate to `Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile`. Set `Inbound connections` to `Block (default)`. Apply the GPO to the relevant Organizational Units (OUs).
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound action to block for the Domain Profile significantly hardens the system by preventing unsolicited connections, including RDP, unless explicitly allowed by a specific rule. This aligns with control 9.1.2 and 9.1.2 (L1).

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpmc.msc). Navigate to `Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile`. Set `Inbound connections` to `Block (default)`. Apply the GPO to the relevant Organizational Units (OUs).
steps.explanation: Similar to the Domain Profile, blocking inbound connections by default for the Public Profile provides a strong security posture, especially for systems that might transition to a public network. This aligns with control 9.3.2 and CIS 9.3.2.

steps.title: Create Specific Windows Firewall Rule for Authorized RDP (if needed)
steps.command_or_action: If RDP access is required, create a new inbound rule in Windows Firewall with Advanced Security. Set `Action` to `Allow the connection`. Set `Protocols and Ports` to `TCP` and `Specific local ports` to `3389`. Under `Scope`, restrict `Remote IP address` to only trusted source IPs (e.g., 10.128.1.164 if it's an authorized source, or a specific management subnet). Apply to `Domain` and `Private` profiles as appropriate.
steps.explanation: After blocking all inbound connections by default, this step ensures that legitimate RDP access is still possible but is strictly controlled to known, authorized sources, minimizing exposure to brute-force attacks (T1110) and unauthorized access.

steps.title: Ensure RDP Event Logging is Enabled and Monitored
steps.command_or_action: Verify that RDP logging is enabled. In Event Viewer, navigate to "Applications and Services Logs" -> "Microsoft" -> "Windows" -> "TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager" -> "Operational" and "TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager" -> "Operational". Ensure these logs are configured to retain sufficient history and are forwarded to a central SIEM or log management solution for real-time monitoring and alerting.
steps.explanation: Continuous monitoring of RDP logs is crucial for detecting and responding to unauthorized access attempts or suspicious activity, directly supporting NIST-SI-04's system monitoring requirements.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the affected Windows Server/Windows 11 systems.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if managing firewall settings via GPO.
Network access to perform RDP connection tests.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. **Firewall Configuration:** On a target system, open `wf.msc` (Windows Firewall with Advanced Security). Verify that for both `Domain Profile` and `Public Profile`, `Inbound connections` is set to `Block (default)`. If an RDP allow rule was created, confirm its scope (source IPs, ports, profiles) is correctly configured. 2. **RDP Access Test:** From an *unauthorized* IP address, attempt to initiate an RDP connection to the target system (e.g., `mstsc /v:192.168.220.132`). The connection should fail. From an *authorized* IP address (if an allow rule was created), attempt an RDP connection to confirm it succeeds. 3. **Log Review:** After attempting connections, check the Event Viewer logs (`TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager` and `TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager`) on the target system to confirm that connection attempts (both successful and blocked) are being logged.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. steps.title: Enable detailed RDP connection logging
steps.command_or_action:
Open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following command to ensure detailed logon/logoff auditing is enabled:
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Logon" /success:enable /failure:enable
steps.explanation: This command enhances the logging of RDP connection attempts, both successful and failed, in the Windows Security Event Log. This is crucial for detecting brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) by identifying multiple failed login attempts and for monitoring unauthorized successful connections (MITRE ATT&CK T1563.002). This aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to monitor for attacks and unauthorized connections.

steps.title: Restrict RDP access via Windows Firewall and verify public profile settings
steps.command_or_action:
1. Verify Network Profile: Open PowerShell as Administrator and run `Get-NetConnectionProfile`. Identify the active network profile (e.g., Domain, Private, Public) for the network adapter receiving RDP connections.
2. Restrict RDP Inbound Rule: If the active profile is *not* Public, open `wf.msc` (Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security). Navigate to `Inbound Rules`. Locate the `Remote Desktop (TCP-In)` rule. Right-click and select `Properties`. Go to the `Scope` tab. Under `Remote IP address`, select `These IP addresses` and add only the specific IP addresses or subnets that are authorized to initiate RDP connections. For example, to allow only `10.128.1.176` and `10.128.1.177`:
   `Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Remote Desktop (TCP-In)" -RemoteAddress "10.128.1.176,10.128.1.177"`
   If the observed `10.128.1.176` is unauthorized and needs to be blocked specifically while others are allowed, ensure the existing RDP rule is scoped correctly, and if necessary, create a specific block rule:
   `New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Unauthorized RDP Source 10.128.1.176" -Direction Inbound -Action Block -RemoteAddress 10.128.1.176 -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 3389`
3. Ensure Public Profile Block (CIS 9.3.2): If the active network profile is `Public`, or as a general hardening measure, ensure inbound connections are blocked by default for the Public profile. This can be checked in `Windows Defender Firewall` -> `Change notification settings` -> `Public network settings`. The setting `Block all incoming connections, including those in the list of allowed apps` should be enabled for the Public profile.
steps.explanation: Limiting RDP access to only authorized source IP addresses significantly reduces the attack surface. This directly mitigates brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) and prevents unauthorized remote access attempts, including potential RDP session hijacking (MITRE ATT&CK T1563.002), by blocking connections from untrusted sources. Ensuring the Public network profile blocks inbound connections by default (as per CIS 9.3.2) provides a robust defense if the network profile ever incorrectly switches to Public. This aligns with CIS 9.3.2 and NIST-SI-04 by preventing unauthorized network connections.

steps.title: Configure account lockout policy for RDP users
steps.command_or_action:
Open `secpol.msc` (Local Security Policy). Navigate to `Account Policies` -> `Account Lockout Policy`. Configure the following settings:
- `Account lockout threshold`: Set to `3-5 invalid logon attempts`.
- `Account lockout duration`: Set to `30 minutes` or more.
- `Reset account lockout counter after`: Set to `30 minutes` or more.
For domain-joined machines, these settings should be configured via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: An account lockout policy automatically disables an account after a specified number of failed login attempts. This is a critical control against brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) on RDP, preventing attackers from continuously guessing passwords. This aligns with NIST-SI-04's objective to detect and prevent attacks.

steps.title: Review and disconnect suspicious RDP sessions
steps.command_or_action:
To list active RDP sessions: `query user` or `quser`
To disconnect a specific session (replace `<SessionID>` with the ID from `query user`): `logoff <SessionID>`
steps.explanation: Immediately reviewing and disconnecting any unauthorized or suspicious RDP sessions helps to contain potential ongoing compromise and prevents further unauthorized activity, directly addressing the observed suspicious connection. This is a direct response to potential RDP session hijacking (MITRE ATT&CK T1563.002) and aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to analyze detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Restrict RDP traffic at the network perimeter
steps.command_or_action:
Log in to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface. Navigate to `Policies` -> `Security`. Create a new security rule or modify an existing one to:
- `Source Zone`: Specify internal zones where RDP is legitimately initiated.
- `Destination Zone`: Specify zones containing RDP servers.
- `Application`: `ms-rdp`
- `Service/URL Category`: `application-default` or `tcp/3389`
- `Action`: `allow`
Ensure there is a broader `deny` rule for RDP from unauthorized source zones or to unauthorized destination zones. Specifically, if the observed connection `10.128.1.176 to 192.168.220.132` is unauthorized, ensure that the firewall rules explicitly block this specific source-destination pair for RDP.
steps.explanation: Implementing network-level restrictions on the Palo Alto Firewall provides an additional layer of defense, controlling RDP traffic flow across network segments. This prevents unauthorized RDP connections from reaching target systems, mitigating brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) and RDP session hijacking (MITRE ATT&CK T1563.002) at the network perimeter. This aligns with NIST-SI-04's recommendation for strategic monitoring and control at network boundaries.

prerequisites: Administrator access to Windows Server/11 systems
Administrator access to Palo Alto Firewall
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the remediation steps, verify the following:
1. Check Windows Security Event Logs for RDP connection attempts (Event IDs 4624, 4625) to confirm detailed logging is active.
2. Attempt RDP connections from unauthorized IP addresses to confirm they are blocked by the Windows Firewall.
3. Attempt RDP connections with incorrect credentials multiple times to verify that the account lockout policy is functioning as expected.
4. Review Palo Alto Firewall logs to confirm RDP traffic is being filtered according to the new or modified rules.

control.control_id: 18.3.4 (L1) Ensure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' is set to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'

Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Domain: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'

control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Block (default): Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections
Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Open an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt and run: reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" /v NodeType /t REG_DWORD /d 2 /f
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (0x2) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution relies solely on WINS servers, reducing broadcast traffic and the potential for NetBIOS enumeration. This makes it harder for attackers to gather information about the network, which can be a precursor to brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001). This addresses control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Editor.
2. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.
3. Locate and double-click 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
4. Set the policy to 'Disabled'.
5. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
6. Run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt to apply the changes immediately.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions. This reduces the attack surface for reconnaissance and unauthorized access attempts, which are often precursors to brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001). This addresses control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Block inbound connections for the Domain Profile in Windows Firewall
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Editor.
2. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties.
3. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab.
4. Under 'State', ensure 'Firewall state' is 'On (recommended)'.
5. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
6. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
7. Run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt to apply the changes immediately.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Domain Profile prevents unauthorized RDP connections (port 3389) from reaching the server. This directly mitigates brute force (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized remote access attempts by preventing the initial connection. This addresses controls 9.1.2 and 9.1.2 (L1).

steps.title: Block inbound connections for the Public Profile in Windows Firewall
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Editor.
2. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties.
3. Select the 'Public Profile' tab.
4. Under 'State', ensure 'Firewall state' is 'On (recommended)'.
5. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
6. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
7. Run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt to apply the changes immediately.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Public Profile provides an additional layer of security, preventing unauthorized RDP connections (port 3389) if the server is ever exposed to a less trusted network. This further mitigates brute force (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized remote access. This addresses control CIS 9.3.2.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the Windows Server
Group Policy Management Console (if applying via GPO to multiple machines)
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. Verify NetBT NodeType: Open an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt and run 'reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters /v NodeType'. Confirm 'NodeType' is '0x2'.
2. Verify 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback': Open Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc), navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options', and confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'.
3. Verify Windows Firewall settings: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security', navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties', and confirm that for both 'Domain Profile' and 'Public Profile', 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, check the registry key 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction' and 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\PublicProfile:DefaultInboundAction' are set to '1' (Block).

findings.title: Connection to Suspicious Port: SSH (22)
findings.description: Connection from 10.128.2.109 to 10.100.104.66 on port 22 (SSH). This port is commonly associated with attacks or services that should be monitored.

metadata.technical_summary: A connection from 10.128.2.109 to 10.100.104.66 on port 22 (SSH) was observed, indicating potential suspicious activity or a service that requires monitoring.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. Boundary Protection

Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system; Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; and Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary). communications at external managed interfaces to the system are monitored; communications at external managed interfaces to the system are controlled; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are monitored; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are controlled; subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; external networks or systems are only connected to through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Least Functionality

Configure the system to provide only [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Prohibit or restrict the use of the following functions, ports, protocols, software, and/or services: [ASSIGNMENT]. Systems provide a wide variety of functions and services. Some of the functions and services routinely provided by default may not be necessary to support essential organizational missions, functions, or operations. Additionally, it is sometimes convenient to provide multiple services from a single system component, but doing so increases risk over limiting the services provided by that single component. Where feasible, organizations limit component functionality to a single function per component. Organizations consider removing unused or unnecessary software and disabling unused or unnecessary physical and logical ports and protocols to prevent unauthorized connection of components, transfer of information, and tunneling. Organizations employ network scanning tools, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and end-point protection technologies, such as firewalls and host-based intrusion detection systems, to identify and prevent the use of prohibited functions, protocols, ports, and services. Least functionality can also be achieved as part of the fundamental design and development of the system (see [SA-8](#sa-8), [SC-2](#sc-2) , and [SC-3](#sc-3)). the system is configured to provide only [ASSIGNMENT]; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted. steps.title: Investigate SSH Connection Legitimacy
steps.command_or_action: Review logs on the target host 10.100.104.66 (if accessible) and network device logs (e.g., Palo Alto Firewall) for connections from 10.128.2.109 to 10.100.104.66 on port 22. Determine the purpose and legitimacy of this connection. Consult with asset owners if necessary.
steps.explanation: Understanding if the observed SSH connection is authorized is critical before implementing changes to avoid service disruption. This initial investigation helps confirm if the activity is malicious, unauthorized, or a legitimate but unmonitored service. (NIST-SI-04)
steps.vendor_product: Ubuntu Linux, Windows Server, Palo Alto Firewall

steps.title: Configure Permitted IP Addresses for SSH Management on Palo Alto Firewall
steps.command_or_action: 1. Log in to the Palo Alto Networks Firewall web interface. 2. Navigate to Device > Setup > Management. 3. Under 'Management Interface Settings', locate the 'Permitted IP Addresses' section for SSH. 4. Ensure that only authorized IP addresses or networks are listed for SSH access. If 10.128.2.109 is not an authorized management IP, remove it or ensure it is not included in the permitted list. 5. Commit the changes.
steps.explanation: This step directly implements control 1.2.2 to limit SSH access to management interfaces, reducing the attack surface for brute force (T1110) and unauthorized access (T1041, T1046). It also aligns with boundary protection principles (NIST-SC-07).

steps.title: Disable or Restrict SSH Service on Target Host 10.100.104.66
steps.command_or_action:
Based on the operating system of 10.100.104.66 and the outcome of Step 1:

If 10.100.104.66 is an Ubuntu Linux server and SSH is not required:
1. Connect to the Ubuntu Linux server 10.100.104.66 via console or an authorized SSH session.
2. To stop the SSH service: sudo systemctl stop ssh
3. To disable the SSH service from starting automatically on boot: sudo systemctl disable ssh

If 10.100.104.66 is an Ubuntu Linux server and SSH is required, but needs restriction:
1. Edit the SSH daemon configuration file: sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
2. Add or modify directives such as 'AllowUsers user1 user2', 'AllowGroups sshusers', or 'ListenAddress 10.100.104.66' to restrict access to specific users, groups, or network interfaces.
3. Restart the SSH service to apply changes: sudo systemctl restart ssh

If 10.100.104.66 is a Windows Server and OpenSSH Server is not required:
1. Connect to the Windows Server 10.100.104.66 via RDP or console.
2. Open 'Services' (services.msc).
3. Locate 'OpenSSH SSH Server' service.
4. Right-click, select 'Properties'.
5. Set 'Startup type' to 'Disabled'.
6. Click 'Stop' if the service is running.
7. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'.

If 10.100.104.66 is a Windows Server and OpenSSH Server is required, but needs restriction:
1. Configure Windows Firewall rules to only allow inbound port 22 connections from authorized source IP addresses or networks. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' > 'Inbound Rules' and create or modify a rule for port 22.
steps.explanation: Disabling unnecessary services and restricting access to essential services reduces the attack surface, aligning with the principle of least functionality (NIST-CM-07). This mitigates unauthorized access attempts (T1110, T1041, T1046) by ensuring SSH is only available when and where it is absolutely needed, and only to authorized sources.

steps.title: Enhance Network Monitoring for Unauthorized SSH Connections
steps.command_or_action: 1. Review existing monitoring configurations on Palo Alto Firewall and other network monitoring tools (e.g., SIEM). 2. Ensure alerts are configured for failed SSH login attempts, successful SSH logins from unusual sources, or high volumes of SSH traffic to/from critical assets. 3. Specifically, configure alerts for any SSH connections originating from 10.128.2.109 to 10.100.104.66 on port 22.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring (NIST-SI-04) helps detect and respond to potential attacks (T1110, T1041, T1046) and unauthorized activity in real-time, providing early warning of suspicious behavior.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface.
Administrative access (console, RDP, or authorized SSH) to the target host 10.100.104.66.
Network diagrams or asset inventory to identify the operating system and role of 10.100.104.66.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt to establish an SSH connection from an unauthorized IP address to 10.100.104.66. The connection should be blocked or refused. 2. If SSH was disabled, verify the SSH service is stopped and disabled on the target host (e.g., 'sudo systemctl status ssh' on Ubuntu, 'services.msc' on Windows). 3. Review Palo Alto Firewall logs to confirm that SSH connections from unauthorized sources are being blocked and that new monitoring alerts are functioning as expected.

steps.title: Investigate the observed SSH connection
steps.command_or_action: Review logs on the source (10.128.2.109) and destination (192.168.220.66) systems, and any intermediate network devices (e.g., Palo Alto Firewall, Cisco Switch) for details regarding the SSH connection. Determine the legitimacy and purpose of the connection. If the connection is unauthorized, proceed with blocking.
steps.explanation: Understanding the context of the connection is crucial before taking action, aligning with NIST-SI-04 to analyze detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Restrict SSH access via Palo Alto Firewall management profiles or security policies
steps.command_or_action: 1. Log in to the Palo Alto Networks Firewall web interface. 2. Navigate to Device > Setup > Management. 3. Under 'Management Profile', select the profile applied to the interface where 192.168.220.66 is located or managed. 4. Ensure 'Permitted IP Addresses' for SSH is configured to allow only authorized source IPs. If 10.128.2.109 is unauthorized, remove or ensure it is not listed. 5. Alternatively, create or modify a Security Policy rule to explicitly deny SSH (port 22) traffic from 10.128.2.109 to 192.168.220.66.
steps.explanation: This prevents unauthorized SSH access to network devices and internal systems by enforcing strict access controls, aligning with Palo Alto control 1.2.2 and NIST-SC-07.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block unauthorized inbound SSH
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows system (192.168.220.66), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Public -InboundAction Block; New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Unauthorized SSH Inbound" -Direction Inbound -LocalPort 22 -Protocol TCP -Action Block -RemoteAddress 10.128.2.109
steps.explanation: This ensures that the Windows Firewall blocks unsolicited inbound connections, including SSH from the identified suspicious source, adhering to Windows control 9.3.2.

steps.title: Ensure NetBT NodeType configuration on Windows Server
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server (192.168.220.66), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" -Name "NodeType" -Value 4 -Force; Restart-Service NetBT
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to P-node (value 4) minimizes NetBIOS broadcast traffic, reducing the attack surface on Windows Servers, as recommended by Windows Server control 18.3.4 (L1). While not directly related to SSH, it's a general hardening measure for Windows Servers.

steps.title: Enhance system monitoring for unauthorized SSH connections
steps.command_or_action: Ensure that your SIEM or logging solution is configured to alert on SSH connections from unexpected sources or to sensitive internal systems. Specifically, configure alerts for SSH traffic originating from 10.128.2.109 to 192.168.220.66, and review existing monitoring rules for SSH activity.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring helps detect and respond to future unauthorized access attempts, fulfilling the requirements of NIST-SI-04 for continuous system monitoring.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Palo Alto Networks Firewall.
Administrative access to the system at 192.168.220.66 (if Windows Server/11).
Access to network device logs (e.g., Cisco Switch) and SIEM/logging solution.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, attempt an SSH connection from 10.128.2.109 to 192.168.220.66. The connection should be blocked. Verify that new alerts are generated in your SIEM for any unauthorized SSH attempts. Review Palo Alto Firewall logs to confirm the blocking rule is active and logging denied connections.

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy Management Console (GPMC), navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Apply this GPO to the relevant Windows Server and Windows 11 organizational units.
steps.explanation: This action implements a default-deny posture for all inbound network connections, significantly reducing the attack surface by preventing unauthorized RDP connections and other services from being exposed. This directly addresses the potential for suspicious activity on port 3389.

steps.title: Review and Restrict Existing RDP Firewall Rules (if RDP is required)
steps.command_or_action: If RDP access is legitimately required for specific systems, create explicit inbound firewall rules that permit RDP (port 3389) ONLY from authorized source IP addresses or subnets. Ensure these rules are as restrictive as possible and do not allow 'Any' source IP. Remove any overly permissive RDP inbound rules.
steps.explanation: While the default inbound connections are blocked, specific RDP rules might exist. Reviewing and restricting these rules ensures that RDP is only accessible from trusted locations, mitigating brute-force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized access attempts.

steps.title: Enhance System Monitoring for RDP Connection Attempts
steps.command_or_action: Ensure that Windows Security Auditing is configured to log successful and failed RDP connection attempts (Event ID 4624 for successful logon, 4625 for failed logon, 4634 for logoff). Configure your Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system to ingest and alert on these events, especially for repeated failed attempts or connections from unusual source IPs. Refer to NIST-SI-04 for detailed monitoring objectives.
steps.explanation: Even with firewall rules in place, continuous monitoring is essential to detect and respond to any attempts to bypass controls or identify new suspicious activity. This aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to monitor for attacks and unauthorized connections, providing visibility into potential brute-force attempts (T1110, T1110.001).

prerequisites: Administrative access to target Windows Server and Windows 11 systems.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) with permissions to modify GPOs.
Access to Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system for log configuration and review.
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. On a target Windows Server or Windows 11 system, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc) and verify that the 'Inbound connections' setting for the 'Domain Profile' is set to 'Block (default)'.
2. Attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address to a remediated system and confirm that the connection is blocked.
3. Verify that RDP connection attempts (both successful and failed) are being logged in the Windows Security Event Log (Event IDs 4624, 4625) and are forwarded to the SIEM.

steps.title: Investigate Observed RDP Connection
steps.command_or_action: Review security event logs on 192.168.220.132 (Windows Server) for Event ID 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) originating from 10.128.1.176 around the time of the observed connection. Check RDP-specific logs under "Applications and Services Logs" -> "Microsoft" -> "Windows" -> "TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager" -> "Operational" and "TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager" -> "Operational".
steps.explanation: This step is crucial to determine if the observed RDP connection was legitimate, an attempted brute-force, or a successful unauthorized access. It directly addresses the 'potential suspicious activity' and aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to 'Analyze detected events and anomalies'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Public Connections
steps.command_or_action: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. In the left pane, click 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Public Profile' tab. Under 'State', ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. Alternatively, use PowerShell: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -DefaultInboundAction Block
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the public profile by default significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unauthorized RDP access from external networks. This directly addresses CIS 9.3.2 and 9.3.2.

steps.title: Set NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Open the Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters. Locate or create a DWORD (32-bit) Value named NodeType. Set its value data to 2 (P-node). Restart the NetBIOS over TCP/IP service or the system for the change to take effect.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (Point-to-Point) configures the system to use a NetBIOS Name Server (WINS) for name resolution, reducing reliance on broadcast-based name resolution. This minimizes the risk of NetBIOS name spoofing and other related attacks, improving overall network security. This addresses control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Disable Network Security LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc). Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options'. Locate and double-click 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'. Set the policy to 'Disabled'. Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. Alternatively, use the Registry Editor: Open regedit.exe. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa. Locate or create a DWORD (32-bit) Value named AllowLsaNullSessionFallback. Set its value data to 0. Restart the system for the change to take effect.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from accessing resources that are configured to allow NULL sessions, even if the LocalSystem account is used. This mitigates a potential vector for information disclosure and unauthorized access, enhancing the security posture of the Windows Server. This addresses control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Secure Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Access
steps.command_or_action: 1. Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA): Open 'System Properties' -> 'Remote' tab. Check 'Allow connections only from computers running Remote Desktop with Network Level Authentication (recommended)'. 2. Restrict RDP access to specific users/groups: In 'System Properties' -> 'Remote' tab, click 'Select Users...' and ensure only authorized users/groups are listed. 3. Implement strong password policies and account lockout thresholds: Configure these via Group Policy (gpedit.msc) under 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Account Policies'. 4. Consider using a VPN or jump box: If RDP access is required from outside the internal network, enforce connection through a VPN or a hardened jump box, rather than direct internet exposure.
steps.explanation: These measures significantly reduce the risk of brute-force attacks (T1110) and unauthorized RDP access by requiring stronger authentication and limiting the attack surface. This aligns with NIST-SI-04's objective of monitoring and preventing attacks.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the Windows Server (192.168.220.132)
Network access to the server
Access to Group Policy Editor or Registry Editor
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying firewall changes, attempt to connect to 192.168.220.132:3389 from an unauthorized external host. The connection should be blocked. Verify NetBT NodeType by running 'ipconfig /all' in a command prompt and checking the 'Node Type' for network adapters (it should be 'P-node' or 'Hybrid'). Re-check the Group Policy setting or registry key for 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to confirm it is 'Disabled' or set to '0'. Verify RDP security settings by confirming NLA is enabled and only authorized users can connect.

steps.title: Block Inbound Connections on Windows Firewall Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action blocks all incoming connections on the Domain network profile, including RDP (port 3389), preventing unauthorized access and mitigating brute-force attempts (T1110) against the RDP service.

steps.title: Block Inbound Connections on Windows Firewall Public Profile
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action blocks all incoming connections on the Public network profile, including RDP (port 3389), preventing unauthorized access and mitigating brute-force attempts (T1110) against the RDP service, especially if the server is exposed to public networks.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Administrative Templates\Network\NetBIOS over TCP/IP' in Group Policy Management Editor. Set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled' and select 'P-node (recommended)' from the options.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node ensures that NetBIOS name resolution primarily uses a NetBIOS Name Server (NBNS) like WINS, reducing reliance on broadcast queries which can be susceptible to spoofing and information disclosure.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' in Group Policy Management Editor. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when authenticating, which can prevent unauthorized access to system resources via anonymous connections.

prerequisites: Administrative privileges on a Domain Controller or target Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor.
remediation.verification_procedure: Confirm the Group Policy settings for 'Inbound connections' on both Domain and Public profiles are set to 'Block (default)' by navigating to the specified UI paths. Additionally, attempt an RDP connection from the source IP (10.128.1.176) to the target (10.100.104.132) and verify it is blocked. Verify the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' is set to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)' and 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is set to 'Disabled' in Group Policy.
