best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. steps.title: Investigate the observed RDP connection
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs (Security log, Event ID 4624 for successful logons, 4625 for failed logons) on 192.168.220.132 for the connection from 10.128.1.176. Check firewall logs on the Palo Alto Firewall for details on the connection. Determine if the connection was authorized, by whom, and for what purpose.
steps.explanation: Understanding the nature of the RDP connection is critical to determine if it was legitimate or an indicator of compromise. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for analyzing detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block unauthorized inbound RDP connections
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Public -InboundAction Block. Then, ensure that any necessary RDP inbound rules are explicitly defined and restricted to known, authorized source IP addresses or subnets. For example, to allow RDP only from a specific IP: New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow RDP from Authorized IP" -Direction Inbound -LocalPort 3389 -Protocol TCP -Action Allow -RemoteAddress '10.128.1.176' (replace with actual authorized IP).
steps.explanation: Blocking all inbound connections by default on the Public profile and only allowing RDP from specific, authorized sources significantly reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized access attempts, addressing controls CIS 9.3.2 and 9.3.2.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" -Name "NodeType" -Value 0x2 -Force. After applying, a reboot may be required for the change to take full effect.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (0x2) configures NetBIOS over TCP/IP to use a WINS server for name resolution, minimizing broadcast traffic and reducing the potential for NetBIOS name spoofing or enumeration, which is a general hardening recommendation as per control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server, open PowerShell as Administrator and execute: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" -Name "AllowLsaNullSessionFallback" -Value 0 -Force. A reboot may be required.
steps.explanation: Disabling 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' prevents anonymous users from accessing named pipes or shares as the LocalSystem account. This mitigates potential privilege escalation and information disclosure vulnerabilities, aligning with control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Enhance RDP connection monitoring and alerting
steps.command_or_action: Ensure that Windows Event Forwarding is configured to collect Security Event IDs 4624 (successful logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4648 (explicit credential logon), and 4776 (credential validation) from all RDP-enabled servers. Integrate these logs into your SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system and configure alerts for multiple failed RDP logon attempts from a single source IP, or successful RDP logons from unusual source IPs or at unusual times.
steps.explanation: Proactive and centralized monitoring of RDP activity is crucial for detecting brute-force attacks (T1110) or suspicious access attempts in real-time. This directly supports the objectives of NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring and attack detection.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the affected Windows Server (192.168.220.132)
Access to Palo Alto Firewall management interface
Access to SIEM for log review and alert configuration
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the remediation steps, verify the following:
1. Attempt to RDP to 192.168.220.132 from an unauthorized IP address; the connection should be blocked by the firewall.
2. On the Windows Server, open Registry Editor (regedit.exe) and navigate to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters. Verify that 'NodeType' is set to '0x2'.
3. Navigate to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa. Verify that 'AllowLsaNullSessionFallback' is set to '0'.
4. Monitor your SIEM for RDP-related alerts and confirm that logs from the RDP server are being ingested correctly.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default (Domain Profile)
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections in Group Policy Management Editor and set to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This configuration prevents unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP, reducing the attack surface and mitigating brute-force attempts (MITRE T1110, T1110.001). This aligns with the principle of least privilege for network access.

steps.title: Configure System Monitoring for RDP and Authentication Events
steps.command_or_action: Ensure Windows Event Logging is configured to audit successful and failed logon attempts (e.g., Event IDs 4624 for successful, 4625 for failed) and RDP connection events (e.g., Event ID 1149 for RDP successful connection). These audit policies can be configured via Group Policy under Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Advanced Audit Policy Configuration\Audit Policies.
steps.explanation: Enhanced monitoring helps detect and log suspicious RDP activity, brute-force attempts, and unauthorized access, aligning with NIST-SI-04 for detecting attacks and unauthorized connections. This provides crucial data for incident response.

prerequisites: Administrative access to target Windows Servers/Clients
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (if applying via GPO)
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. For Windows Firewall: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor and confirm 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction and confirm its value indicates 'Block'. 2. For System Monitoring: On a target system, open Event Viewer and navigate to 'Windows Logs' -> 'Security'. Filter for Event IDs 4624, 4625, and 1149 to confirm that these events are being logged. Attempt a failed RDP login from an unauthorized source and verify that Event ID 4625 is recorded.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (secpol.msc). Go to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This step configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default for the Domain Profile, significantly reducing the attack surface and preventing unauthorized RDP connections, which aligns with preventing brute force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Prevent anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (secpol.msc). Go to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shared folders. This is crucial as attackers often use this information to gather valid usernames before attempting brute-force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Restrict anonymous users from having 'Everyone' permissions
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (secpol.msc). Go to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group. This limits their access to system resources and reduces the potential for privilege escalation or information disclosure that could aid in unauthorized access attempts.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (secpol.msc). Go to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the system from allowing anonymous connections to access sensitive information, which could be exploited during reconnaissance phases of an attack, potentially leading to brute force attempts.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) or Local Security Policy Editor (secpol.msc).
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the Group Policy settings, force a Group Policy update by running 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated command prompt. Then, verify each setting:
1. For Windows Firewall, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' and confirm 'Inbound connections' is 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction.
2. For anonymous SAM enumeration, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and confirm 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is 'Enabled'. Alternatively, check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous.
3. For anonymous 'Everyone' permissions, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and confirm 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is 'Disabled'. Alternatively, check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous.
4. For NULL session fallback, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

steps.title: Investigate Suspicious RDP Connection on Target Server
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server (10.100.104.132), open Event Viewer and review the Security and System logs. Look for Event ID 4624 (successful logon) or 4625 (failed logon) in the Security log, specifically for 'Logon Type 10' (RemoteInteractive) around the time of the alert, originating from IP address 10.128.1.176. You can use PowerShell: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Security' | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 4624 -or $_.Id -eq 4625} | Where-Object {$_.Message -like '*RemoteInteractive*' -and $_.Message -like '*10.128.1.176*'}
steps.explanation: This step helps determine if the suspicious RDP connection was successful, who initiated it, and if it was legitimate or malicious. This aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to 'Analyze detected events and anomalies' and identify 'unauthorized use of the system'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy (GPO) or local policy on the Windows Server (10.100.104.132), navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections. Set this option to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound action to 'Block' ensures that all incoming connections, including RDP, are blocked unless explicitly allowed by a specific firewall rule. This significantly reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized network connections, as per control 9.1.2.

steps.title: Review and Restrict Existing RDP Firewall Rules
steps.command_or_action: In Windows Firewall with Advanced Security on the target server (10.100.104.132), navigate to 'Inbound Rules'. Locate any rules that allow RDP (port 3389). Edit these rules to restrict the 'Remote IP address' scope to only trusted source IPs or subnets. If RDP is required and no specific rule exists, create a new inbound rule allowing RDP (port 3389) only from explicitly authorized source IP addresses or ranges.
steps.explanation: If RDP access is necessary, restricting its availability to only authorized source IP addresses minimizes the risk of unauthorized access and brute-force attacks (MITRE T1110) against the RDP service, aligning with NIST-SI-04's objective to detect unauthorized network connections.

steps.title: Enhance Network Monitoring for RDP Traffic on Palo Alto Firewall
steps.command_or_action: Log in to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface. Configure security policies to log and alert on RDP (port 3389) connections originating from or destined for internal networks, especially from unusual source IPs or to sensitive servers. Specifically, ensure logging is enabled for traffic from 10.128.1.176 to 10.100.104.132 on port 3389, and review existing logs for this specific connection.
steps.explanation: Proactive network monitoring at the firewall level helps detect and respond to suspicious RDP activity in real-time, providing an external monitoring capability as described in NIST-SI-04 to identify unauthorized network connections and potential attacks.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (10.100.104.132)
Administrative access to Group Policy Management Console (if managing firewall via GPO)
Administrative access to the Palo Alto Firewall
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, verify the following: 1. On the Windows Server, open 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' and confirm that the 'Domain Profile' (or active profile) has 'Inbound connections' set to 'Block (default)'. 2. Review the 'Inbound Rules' to ensure RDP rules are either disabled or explicitly restricted to authorized source IP addresses. 3. Attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address to the target server and confirm it is blocked. 4. On the Palo Alto Firewall, confirm that logging and alerting are configured for RDP traffic as specified in step 4.

steps.title: Block Inbound RDP Connections on Public Profile
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc) or apply via domain GPO. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Public Profile' tab and ensure 'State: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. If RDP is legitimately required, create a specific inbound rule allowing only authorized source IPs (e.g., 10.128.1.176 if it's a legitimate source) to port 3389, and ensure the default 'Block' rule is still active for all other unauthorized traffic.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the public profile prevents unauthorized RDP access (port 3389) from external or untrusted networks, directly addressing the observed suspicious connection. This significantly reduces the attack surface and helps mitigate brute-force attacks (T1110) and unauthorized remote access attempts (T1563.002).

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc) or apply via domain GPO. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone', which can limit their ability to enumerate system information or gain unauthorized access. This reduces the attack surface and makes it harder for attackers to prepare for brute-force (T1110) or other remote access exploits.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open Group Policy Management Editor (gpedit.msc) or apply via domain GPO. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents unauthenticated users from establishing NULL sessions to enumerate system information, users, and shares. This reduces the reconnaissance capabilities of attackers, making it harder to prepare for brute-force attacks (T1110) or exploit RDP for unauthorized access (T1563.002).

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action:
On the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open an elevated Command Prompt or PowerShell and execute the following command:
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" /v NodeType /t REG_DWORD /d 2 /f
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) configures NetBIOS over TCP/IP to use WINS servers exclusively for name resolution, minimizing broadcast traffic. This reduces the risk of NetBIOS name spoofing and other network-level attacks that could precede or facilitate unauthorized remote access attempts (T1563.002).

steps.title: Review System Monitoring and Logs for RDP Activity
steps.command_or_action: Review security event logs (Event ID 4624 for successful logins, 4625 for failed logins, 4776 for NTLM authentication) on the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132) for any further unauthorized RDP connection attempts. Ensure your SIEM or monitoring solution (NIST-SI-04) is configured to alert on suspicious RDP activity, especially from unusual source IPs or repeated failed login attempts (T1110).
steps.explanation: Continuous monitoring and log review are critical for detecting and responding to unauthorized remote access attempts (T1563.002) and brute-force attacks (T1110). This step ensures that the system monitoring capabilities are actively used to identify and alert on potential security incidents, as per NIST SP 800-53 SI-4.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132).
Understanding of Group Policy Management (if applying via GPO).
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt to establish an RDP connection from the source IP (10.128.1.176) or any unauthorized IP to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 and confirm it fails. 2. Verify the Group Policy settings for 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' and 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' are set to 'Disabled'. 3. Verify the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType is set to '2'. 4. Review the Windows Security Event Log for any new RDP connection attempts (Event ID 4624/4625) and confirm no unauthorized connections are successful.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. steps.title: Enable RDP Connection Event Logging
steps.command_or_action:
To enable RDP connection logging, execute the following commands in an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt:
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server" /v fDenyTSConnections /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\Winstations\RDP-Tcp" /v fLogonEvents /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
steps.explanation: This step enables detailed logging for RDP connections, allowing administrators to monitor successful and failed login attempts. This is crucial for detecting brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized access attempts, directly addressing the monitoring requirement of NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action:
To configure the Windows Firewall via Group Policy:
1. Open Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC).
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile.
3. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.

Alternatively, for local policy, execute the following command in an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt:
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile" /v DefaultInboundAction /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound action to 'Block' significantly reduces the attack surface by preventing unsolicited connections. This is a fundamental security practice to mitigate unauthorized access, including RDP brute-force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001), aligning with control 9.1.2. Specific RDP rules should then be created to allow legitimate access from trusted sources only.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action:
To disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback via Group Policy:
1. Open Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC).
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.
3. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.

Alternatively, for local policy, execute the following command in an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt:
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" /v AllowLsaNullSessionFallback /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents unauthenticated users from accessing system resources and enumerating information that could be used in further attacks. This is a general hardening measure that reduces the attack surface and makes it harder for attackers to gather intelligence for brute-force or other exploitation attempts, aligning with control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action:
To configure the NetBT NodeType, execute the following command in an elevated PowerShell or Command Prompt:
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" /v NodeType /t REG_DWORD /d 2 /f
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) configures the system to use a WINS server for NetBIOS name resolution, minimizing broadcast traffic. This can reduce the visibility of the system on the local network, making it slightly more difficult for attackers to discover and target it, contributing to overall network security as per control 18.3.4 (L1).

prerequisites: Administrator privileges on the target Windows Server or Windows 11 system.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (GPMC) if applying changes via Group Policy.
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. **RDP Logging:** Check Event Viewer -> Applications and Services Logs -> Microsoft -> Windows -> TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager -> Operational for Event ID 21 (Remote Desktop Services: Session logon succeeded) and 22 (Remote Desktop Services: Shell start notification). Also, check the Security log for Event ID 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) with Logon Type 10 (RemoteInteractive).
2. **Windows Firewall:** Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security', navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties', and confirm 'Domain Profile' -> 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, verify the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction is 1.
3. **NULL Session Fallback:** Verify the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\AllowLsaNullSessionFallback is 0.
4. **NetBT NodeType:** Verify the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\NodeType is 2.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy Editor. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unauthorized RDP connections and mitigating brute-force attempts (T1110) by restricting access to remote services (T1021).

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy Editor. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this policy prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone', which limits their ability to access network resources and reduces the potential for information gathering or unauthorized access attempts.

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy Editor. Set this policy to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this policy prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shared folders, making it harder for attackers to gather valid usernames for brute-force attacks (T1110.001) against RDP.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy Editor. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents potential information disclosure and unauthorized access that could be exploited by attackers attempting to gain a foothold via remote services (T1021).

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 system(s).
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (for domain-wide changes) or Local Security Policy Editor (for local changes).
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the changes, perform the following checks:
1.  **Windows Firewall:** Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' and confirm 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, check the registry at 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction' and confirm its value reflects 'Block'. (Source: 9.1.2)
2.  **Anonymous Permissions:** Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' and confirm it is 'Disabled'. Alternatively, check the registry at 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous' and confirm its value reflects 'Disabled'. (Source: 2.3.10.4)
3.  **Anonymous SAM Enumeration:** Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' and confirm it is 'Enabled'. Alternatively, check the registry at 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous' and confirm its value reflects 'Enabled'. (Source: 2.3.10.3)
4.  **NULL Session Fallback:** Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and confirm it is 'Disabled'. (Source: 2.3.11.2)

Additionally, attempt to establish an RDP connection from an unauthorized source to confirm it is blocked by the firewall.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to Block Inbound Connections via Group Policy
steps.command_or_action: On a Domain Controller or a system with Group Policy Management tools installed, open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab and set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Apply this GPO to the organizational unit (OU) containing the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machines.
steps.explanation: This step ensures that all inbound connections, including RDP (port 3389), are blocked by default when the system is connected to a domain network. This directly mitigates unauthorized access attempts and brute force attacks (MITRE T1110) by preventing external systems from initiating connections.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to Block Inbound Connections via Group Policy
steps.command_or_action: On a Domain Controller or a system with Group Policy Management tools installed, open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Public Profile' tab and set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Apply this GPO to the organizational unit (OU) containing the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machines.
steps.explanation: This step ensures that all inbound connections, including RDP (port 3389), are blocked by default when the system is connected to a public network. This provides an additional layer of defense against unauthorized access attempts and brute force attacks (MITRE T1110) if the system's network profile changes.

steps.title: Force Group Policy Update on Target Systems
steps.command_or_action: On the affected Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, open an elevated Command Prompt or PowerShell and run: gpupdate /force
steps.explanation: This command forces the system to immediately apply the updated Group Policy settings, ensuring the firewall rules are enforced without waiting for the next scheduled policy refresh.

prerequisites: Administrative access to a Domain Controller or a system with Group Policy Management tools.
Local administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine (if applying local policy or forcing gpupdate).
remediation.verification_procedure:
On the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, open an elevated Command Prompt or PowerShell and run the following commands to check the registry settings:
1. For Domain Profile: reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile /v DefaultInboundAction
2. For Public Profile: reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\PublicProfile /v DefaultInboundAction

Confirm that the 'DefaultInboundAction' value for both profiles is '0x0' (which corresponds to 'Block'). Additionally, navigate to 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc) and visually confirm that 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)' for both the Domain and Public profiles.

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. steps.title: Investigate the RDP connection on the Windows Server
steps.command_or_action:
On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following commands to check security and RDP session logs:
Get-WinEvent -LogName "Security" | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 4624 -or $_.Id -eq 4625 -or $_.Id -eq 4648 -or $_.Id -eq 4776} | Format-List -Property TimeCreated, Id, Message
Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational" | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 21 -or $_.Id -eq 24 -or $_.Id -eq 25} | Format-List -Property TimeCreated, Id, Message
steps.explanation: This step implements NIST-SI-04 by analyzing system logs to determine the legitimacy of the RDP connection from 10.128.1.164. It helps identify successful or failed login attempts (T1110 Brute Force) and active RDP sessions (T1563.002 RDP Hijacking), providing crucial context for further remediation.

steps.title: Restrict RDP access via Windows Firewall
steps.command_or_action:
On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following commands:
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Public -Enabled True -DefaultInboundAction Block
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow RDP from Trusted Sources" -Direction Inbound -LocalPort 3389 -Protocol TCP -Action Allow -RemoteAddress <Trusted_IP_Address_or_Range>
steps.explanation: This implements controls 9.1.2 and 9.3.2 by blocking all inbound connections by default and only allowing RDP from known, trusted sources. This significantly reduces the attack surface for brute force (T1110) and RDP hijacking (T1563.002) attempts.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action:
On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following command:
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" -Name "AllowLegacyNullSessionFallback" -Value 0 -Force
steps.explanation: This directly addresses control 2.3.11.2 by preventing potential privilege escalation or information disclosure via NULL sessions, enhancing the overall security posture of the system.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action:
On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132), open PowerShell as Administrator and run the following command:
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" -Name "NodeType" -Value 2 -Force
steps.explanation: This addresses control 18.3.4 by setting the NetBT node type to P-node (Point-to-Point), which relies on a WINS server for name resolution and reduces broadcast traffic, improving network security and efficiency.

steps.title: Configure Palo Alto Firewall for RDP traffic monitoring and blocking
steps.command_or_action: 1. Log in to the Palo Alto Firewall Web UI. 2. Navigate to `Policies > Security`. 3. Create a new security rule or modify an existing one to explicitly log RDP traffic (port 3389) from the source IP 10.128.1.164 to the destination IP 192.168.220.132. Ensure the 'Action' is set to 'Allow' if the connection is legitimate and requires monitoring, or 'Deny' if it is unauthorized. 4. Navigate to `Monitor > Logs > Traffic` to review the RDP connection logs.
steps.explanation: This implements NIST-SI-04 by ensuring RDP traffic is monitored at the network perimeter. If the connection is unauthorized, blocking it prevents further brute force (T1110) or RDP hijacking (T1563.002) attempts.

steps.title: Implement Account Lockout Policy
steps.command_or_action:
On the Windows Server (192.168.220.132):
1. Open `Local Security Policy` (secpol.msc).
2. Navigate to `Account Policies > Account Lockout Policy`.
3. Double-click `Account lockout threshold` and set it to '5 invalid logon attempts'.
4. Double-click `Account lockout duration` and set it to '30 minutes'.
5. Double-click `Reset account lockout counter after` and set it to '30 minutes'.
steps.explanation: This directly mitigates brute force attacks (T1110) by automatically locking out user accounts after a specified number of failed login attempts, preventing attackers from guessing passwords indefinitely.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the Windows Server (192.168.220.132)
Access to the Palo Alto Firewall management interface
RDP client for testing (if needed)
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. On the Windows Server, verify the firewall rules are active by running `Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow RDP from Trusted Sources"`. 2. Verify the registry settings for 'AllowLegacyNullSessionFallback' and 'NodeType' by checking the respective paths in `regedit.exe`. 3. On the Palo Alto Firewall, confirm the security policy for RDP traffic is configured as specified and review traffic logs for any new suspicious RDP connections. 4. Test RDP access from a trusted source and confirm that RDP from untrusted sources is blocked. 5. Attempt to log in with an incorrect password multiple times to verify the account lockout policy is active.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Local Security Policy. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default when the system is on a domain network. This directly mitigates unauthorized RDP connection attempts, including those associated with brute force attacks (T1110), by preventing them from reaching the system.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine, open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Local Security Policy. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default when the system is on a public network. This provides an additional layer of defense against unauthorized RDP connection attempts, especially if the system is ever exposed to a public network, further mitigating brute force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server, open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Local Security Policy. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate and set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback enhances network security by preventing unauthenticated sessions from accessing system resources. This reduces the attack surface and can help prevent certain types of exploits or information disclosure that could aid in brute force attacks (T1110) or other unauthorized access attempts.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Local Security Policy.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Verify Windows Firewall settings: On the target machine, open an elevated PowerShell prompt and run 'Get-NetFirewallProfile -Name Domain,Public | Select-Object Name,DefaultInboundAction'. Confirm that 'DefaultInboundAction' for both 'Domain' and 'Public' profiles is 'Block'. Alternatively, check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction and confirm its value is '1' (Block). 2. Verify NULL Session Fallback setting: On the target machine, open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Local Security Policy and navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is set to 'Disabled'. 3. Attempt to establish an RDP connection from the identified source IP (10.128.1.176) or another external host to the target (10.100.104.132) and confirm that the connection is blocked.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block public inbound connections
steps.command_or_action: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall' -> 'Properties'. For the 'Public Profile' tab, set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This step directly blocks unsolicited inbound connections, including RDP (port 3389), from public networks, thereby mitigating unauthorized remote access attempts (T1021.001, T1021).

steps.title: Disable anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy (GP), set the following UI path to 'Enabled': Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares. Alternatively, set the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous to 1.
steps.explanation: Disabling anonymous enumeration of Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shares prevents attackers from easily discovering valid usernames, which is a common precursor to brute-force attacks (T1110) against services like RDP (T1021.001).

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled' via Group Policy or Local Security Policy.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources with LocalSystem privileges, reducing the attack surface and potential for information disclosure that could aid in remote access attacks (T1021, T1021.001).

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the changes, verify the settings:1. For Windows Firewall, navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' -> 'Windows Defender Firewall' -> 'Properties' and confirm 'Public Profile' 'Inbound connections' is 'Block (default)'.2. For SAM enumeration, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' in Group Policy Editor and confirm 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is 'Enabled'. Alternatively, check the registry value HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous is 1.3. For NULL session fallback, confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled' in Group Policy or Local Security Policy.

steps.title: Deny Guest Logon Through Remote Desktop Services
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\User Rights Assignment\Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services' in Group Policy Editor. Add 'Guests' to this policy.
steps.explanation: This action prevents unauthenticated or guest accounts from logging in via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP), directly mitigating potential brute-force attacks (T1110) and unauthorized access attempts (T1021.001, T1563.002).

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' in Group Policy Editor. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group. This reduces the scope of permissions available to unauthenticated connections, limiting potential information disclosure or unauthorized access vectors.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' in Group Policy Editor. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents the LocalSystem account from falling back to a NULL session when authenticating to network resources. This helps prevent certain types of privilege escalation or information disclosure attacks.

steps.title: Review and Enhance System Monitoring for Unauthorized RDP Activity
steps.command_or_action: Review existing system monitoring configurations on Windows Servers and Palo Alto Firewalls. Ensure that logging is enabled for RDP connections (port 3389) and failed login attempts. Configure alerts for suspicious RDP activity, such as multiple failed logins from a single source IP address, connections from unusual geographic locations, or connections outside of normal business hours.
steps.explanation: The observed connection indicates a need for robust monitoring. Enhancing monitoring capabilities helps detect and respond to unauthorized RDP access (T1021.001, T1563.002) and brute-force attempts (T1110) in real-time, aligning with NIST-SI-04 for continuous monitoring and incident response.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server machines.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (if applying via GPO) or Local Group Policy Editor (if applying locally).
Access to Palo Alto Firewall management interface.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. For steps 1, 2, and 3, navigate to the respective Group Policy UI paths and confirm that the settings are configured as prescribed (e.g., 'Guests' is added to 'Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services', 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is 'Disabled', and 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'). 2. For step 4, confirm that RDP connection logs and failed login attempts are being collected and reviewed on Windows Servers and Palo Alto Firewalls. Verify that alerts are configured and functioning for suspicious RDP activity.

steps.title: Block unauthorized inbound connections via Windows Firewall
steps.command_or_action: To configure via Group Policy, navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, via PowerShell: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain -DefaultInboundAction Block
steps.explanation: Blocking unauthorized inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unsolicited access to services like RDP. This directly mitigates brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) by preventing attackers from reaching the RDP service.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Open PowerShell as Administrator and run: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters" -Name "NodeType" -Value 2 -Force
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (Point-to-Point) configures the system to use a NetBIOS Name Server (NBNS) for name resolution, reducing broadcast traffic. This makes it harder for attackers to enumerate hosts via NetBIOS broadcasts, which can be a precursor to targeted attacks.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: To configure via Group Policy, navigate to: Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'. Alternatively, via PowerShell: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" -Name "AllowLsaNullSessionFallback" -Value 0 -Force
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents unauthenticated users from accessing system information. This enhances security by limiting information gathering capabilities that attackers might use to prepare for brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) or other forms of unauthorized access.

steps.title: Configure and monitor RDP login attempts
steps.command_or_action: Ensure 'Audit Logon Events' is enabled for both Success and Failure. This can be configured via Group Policy: Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Audit Policy. Set 'Audit logon events' to 'Success, Failure'. To check logs, open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc), navigate to 'Windows Logs' -> 'Security', and filter for Event IDs 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) with 'Logon Type' 10 (RemoteInteractive) for RDP.
steps.explanation: Monitoring RDP login attempts is critical for detecting brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) and unauthorized access attempts. Event ID 4625 indicates failed logons, which can signal an ongoing attack. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for continuous system monitoring.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (if applying changes via Group Policy).
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the steps, verify the following:
1. Confirm the Windows Firewall 'Domain Profile' inbound connections are set to 'Block (default)' via Group Policy or PowerShell.
2. Verify the 'NodeType' registry value under HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters is set to '2'.
3. Verify 'AllowLsaNullSessionFallback' registry value under HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa is set to '0'.
4. Attempt a failed RDP login to the server and confirm that Event ID 4625 (failed logon) is logged in the Security Event Log, with Logon Type 10.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This setting ensures that all incoming connections, including RDP, are blocked by default unless an explicit rule allows them. This significantly reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized RDP connections, aligning with control 9.1.2 (L1).

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions, which can be exploited for reconnaissance and information gathering, a precursor to attacks like MITRE ATT&CK T1110 (Brute Force) against RDP. This addresses control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Administrative Templates\Network\NetBIOS over TCP/IP. Set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node (Point-to-Point) reduces reliance on broadcast-based name resolution, making it harder for attackers to enumerate network resources and potentially reducing the attack surface for NetBIOS-related vulnerabilities. This addresses control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Enable and review RDP connection logging for suspicious activity
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc).
2. Navigate to Applications and Services Logs\Microsoft\Windows\TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager\Operational.
3. Ensure logging is enabled. If not, right-click 'Operational', select 'Properties', and check 'Enable logging'.
4. Regularly review events with Event ID '21' (successful RDP connection) and '22' (RDP shell start) for legitimate connections.
5. For failed login attempts (potential MITRE ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force), review 'Security' logs for Event ID '4625' (failed logon).
steps.explanation: Enhanced logging provides visibility into RDP connection attempts, both successful and failed. Monitoring these logs helps detect unauthorized access attempts, including brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110), and allows for timely incident response, aligning with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Windows Server systems.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (if applying via GPO).
Understanding of Event Viewer.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying Group Policy, run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server and verify the settings are applied. Check the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction to confirm it is set to '1' (Block). Attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP and confirm it is blocked. Verify RDP operational logs in Event Viewer are being generated.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT].
