steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, set the following UI path to 'Block (default)': Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections by default on the Windows Firewall prevents unauthorized remote access attempts, including RDP (MITRE ATT&CK T1021 - Remote Services), thereby reducing the attack surface and preventing potential brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) against services like RDP.

steps.title: Disable anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, set the following UI path to 'Enabled': Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
steps.explanation: Disabling anonymous enumeration prevents attackers from gathering information about user accounts and shared resources, which is a common reconnaissance step before attempting brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) or exploiting remote services (MITRE ATT&CK T1021) via RDP.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' and set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback for LocalSystem prevents unauthorized information disclosure and access, which could be leveraged by attackers to gain a foothold or escalate privileges, contributing to remote service exploitation (MITRE ATT&CK T1021) and potentially aiding brute force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110).

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: To establish the recommended configuration via Group Policy, navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Administrative Templates\Network\NetBIOS over TCP/IP' and set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node (peer-to-peer) reduces network broadcast traffic and relies on WINS servers for name resolution, thereby minimizing the discoverability of systems and reducing the attack surface for network-based reconnaissance that could precede remote service exploitation (MITRE ATT&CK T1021) or brute force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110).

steps.title: Review and enhance RDP monitoring
steps.command_or_action: Review existing system monitoring configurations for RDP (port 3389) on Windows Servers. Ensure that successful and failed RDP login attempts, session durations, and source IP addresses are logged and actively monitored. Consider implementing an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) or Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) rule to alert on multiple failed RDP login attempts from a single source IP within a short timeframe.
steps.explanation: Continuous monitoring of RDP activity is crucial for detecting unauthorized remote access (MITRE ATT&CK T1021) and brute force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110) in real-time. Enhancing monitoring capabilities, as per NIST-SI-04, allows for prompt detection and response to suspicious activities, preventing successful compromise.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Group Policy Management Editor or local security policy on affected Windows Servers.
Access to network monitoring tools (e.g., Palo Alto Firewall logs, SIEM).
remediation.verification_procedure: Attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address and confirm it is blocked. Verify Group Policy settings are applied on target servers using 'gpresult /h result.html'. Check security event logs (Event ID 4625 for failed logins, 4624 for successful logins) on a monitored server to ensure RDP activity is being logged and alerts are triggered for suspicious patterns.

steps.title: Investigate the suspicious RDP connection
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, review Windows Event Logs (Security Log, Event ID 4624 for successful logins, 4625 for failed logins, 4634 for logoffs) for connections originating from 10.128.1.164 around the time of the observed activity. Check for corresponding RDP session events (Event ID 21, 22, 23, 24, 25 from TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager and TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager).
steps.explanation: This step is crucial for understanding the nature of the observed RDP connection. It helps determine if the connection was legitimate, unauthorized, or part of a potential attack, such as T1563.002 (Remote Service: RDP Lateral Movement) or T1110 (Brute Force) if multiple failed attempts are observed. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for monitoring and analyzing detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Review and restrict RDP access permissions
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, open 'Local Group Policy Editor' (gpedit.msc) or 'Group Policy Management' for domain-joined systems. Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'User Rights Assignment'. Ensure 'Allow log on through Remote Desktop Services' is restricted to only authorized users or groups (e.g., 'Administrators', 'Remote Desktop Users'). Remove any unnecessary users or groups.
steps.explanation: Limiting who can connect via RDP reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized access, directly mitigating potential T1563.002 (Remote Service: RDP Lateral Movement) attempts. This is a key aspect of preventing unauthorized remote connections as per NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default for Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Ensure an explicit rule is created for RDP (port 3389) that only allows connections from trusted source IPs or subnets if RDP access is required.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface and prevents unauthorized RDP access, which is a common vector for T1563.002 (Remote Service: RDP Lateral Movement). Explicitly allowing RDP only from trusted sources further hardens the system. This directly implements Windows Control 9.1.2.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound connections by default for Public Profile
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'. Select the 'Public Profile' tab. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. This is especially critical for systems that might be exposed to untrusted networks.
steps.explanation: Similar to the Domain Profile, blocking inbound connections on the Public Profile prevents unauthorized access from external or untrusted networks, mitigating risks associated with T1563.002 (Remote Service: RDP Lateral Movement). This directly implements Windows Control 9.3.2.

steps.title: Enhance RDP connection monitoring and alerting
steps.command_or_action: Ensure your Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system or central logging solution is configured to collect and alert on Windows Event IDs related to RDP: 4624 (successful logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4634 (logoff), and events from 'Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational' (Event ID 21, 24, 25) and 'Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager/Operational' (Event ID 1149). Configure alerts for multiple failed RDP login attempts from a single source IP (indicating T1110 Brute Force) or successful RDP logins from unusual source IPs or times (indicating T1563.002 Lateral Movement).
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring and alerting on RDP events are essential for detecting and responding to suspicious activity, including T1110 (Brute Force) attacks and T1563.002 (Remote Service: RDP Lateral Movement) attempts. This directly implements the monitoring objectives of NIST-SI-04.
steps.vendor_product: Windows Server, Windows 11, Palo Alto Firewall, Cisco Switch

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, open 'Local Group Policy Editor' (gpedit.msc) or 'Group Policy Management'. Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Windows Settings' -> 'Security Settings' -> 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options'. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session, which can be exploited by attackers to gain unauthorized access or information. This is a general hardening measure that reduces the attack surface and mitigates potential vectors for T1563.002. This implements Windows Server Control 2.3.11.2.

steps.title: Configure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to P-node
steps.command_or_action: On the system 192.168.220.132, open 'Local Group Policy Editor' (gpedit.msc) or 'Group Policy Management'. Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' -> 'Administrative Templates' -> 'Network' -> 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'. Locate 'NetBT NodeType configuration' and set it to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node (peer-to-peer) reduces reliance on broadcast-based name resolution, which can be susceptible to spoofing and information disclosure. This improves network security posture and reduces potential attack vectors that could lead to T1563.002. This implements Windows Server Control 18.3.4.

prerequisites: Administrative access to 192.168.220.132 (Windows Server/Windows 11).
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if managing domain-joined systems.
Access to SIEM/logging solution for monitoring configuration.
Network diagrams and understanding of authorized RDP traffic flows.
remediation.verification_procedure: Confirm that the suspicious RDP connection from 10.128.1.164 to 192.168.220.132 has been investigated and its legitimacy determined. Verify that Windows Firewall rules on 192.168.220.132 are configured to block inbound connections by default for Domain and Public profiles, with explicit, restricted rules for RDP if necessary. Confirm that RDP access permissions are restricted to authorized users/groups. Verify that RDP-related events (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4634, 21, 24, 25, 1149) are being collected by the SIEM and that alerts are configured for suspicious RDP activity (e.g., multiple failed logins, logins from unusual sources indicating T1110, or logins from unusual sources/times indicating T1563.002). Confirm that 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled' and 'NetBT NodeType configuration' is 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)' on Windows Server systems.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This action configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default when the system is on a domain network. This significantly reduces the attack surface by preventing unauthorized access attempts, including suspicious RDP connections, and helps mitigate brute-force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This configures the Windows Firewall to block all inbound connections by default when the system is on a public network. This is crucial for protecting against external threats and preventing unauthorized RDP access or brute-force attempts (T1110) from public-facing interfaces.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to 'Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options' in Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set its value to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from accessing system resources via NULL sessions. This helps to prevent reconnaissance activities that could precede an RDP brute-force attack (T1110) by limiting information disclosure.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor if applying changes via Group Policy.
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. For Windows Firewall settings: Open 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc). Navigate to 'Windows Firewall Properties' and confirm that for both 'Domain Profile' and 'Public Profile', the 'Inbound connections' setting is 'Block (default)'.
2. For 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback': Open 'Local Security Policy' (secpol.msc). Navigate to 'Local Policies' -> 'Security Options' and confirm that 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is set to 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Administrative Templates\Network\NetBIOS over TCP/IP. Set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled' and select 'P-node (recommended)' from the dropdown. Via Registry (if not using GPO): Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters. Create or modify a DWORD (32-bit) Value named NodeType and set its data to 2.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution primarily uses WINS servers or directed queries, reducing reliance on broadcast-based name resolution which can be susceptible to spoofing and information disclosure.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'. Via Registry (if not using GPO): Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa. Create or modify a DWORD (32-bit) Value named NullSessionPipes and set its data to 0.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from accessing named pipes or shares as the LocalSystem account, which could otherwise be exploited for information gathering or privilege escalation by an attacker attempting unauthorized access.

steps.title: Block inbound connections on Windows Firewall Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Via Registry (if not using GPO): Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile. Create or modify a DWORD (32-bit) Value named DefaultInboundAction and set its data to 1.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Domain Profile by default significantly reduces the attack surface for systems connected to the domain, preventing unsolicited connections from potentially malicious sources.

steps.title: Block inbound connections on Windows Firewall Public Profile
steps.command_or_action: Via Group Policy: Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Public Profile. Set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Via Registry (if not using GPO): Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\PublicProfile. Create or modify a DWORD (32-bit) Value named DefaultInboundAction and set its data to 1.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on the Public Profile is critical for systems that may be exposed to untrusted networks, ensuring that only explicitly allowed traffic can reach the system.

steps.title: Create Windows Firewall rule to block RDP from unauthorized sources
steps.command_or_action: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Inbound Rules'. Right-click 'Inbound Rules' and select 'New Rule...'. Choose 'Port' and click 'Next'. Select 'TCP' and specify '3389' for 'Specific local ports'. Click 'Next'. Select 'Block the connection'. Click 'Next'. Choose when the rule applies (Domain, Private, Public). Click 'Next'. Give the rule a name (e.g., 'Block RDP from Unauthorized Sources') and an optional description. Click 'Finish'. Alternatively, using PowerShell: New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block RDP from Unauthorized Sources" -Direction Inbound -LocalPort 3389 -Protocol TCP -Action Block -Profile Any
steps.explanation: Explicitly blocking RDP (port 3389) from unauthorized sources at the host firewall level prevents direct remote desktop connection attempts. This directly mitigates brute-force attacks (T1110) and password guessing (T1110.001) by denying attackers the ability to even attempt to log in via RDP.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Windows Server systems
Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) access if applying via Group Policy
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, attempt to establish an RDP connection from an unauthorized host to the remediated system. The connection should be blocked. Verify the firewall rules and Group Policy settings are correctly applied by navigating to the specified paths.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. steps.title: Enable Detailed RDP Connection Logging
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc or manage via GPO).
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Audit Policy.
3. Double-click 'Audit Logon Events' and ensure 'Success' and 'Failure' checkboxes are marked.
4. Open Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc).
5. Navigate to Applications and Services Logs\Microsoft\Windows\TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager\Operational. Right-click and select 'Enable Log' if not already enabled.
6. Navigate to Applications and Services Logs\Microsoft\Windows\TerminalServices-RDPClient\Operational. Right-click and select 'Enable Log' if not already enabled.
steps.explanation: This step implements system monitoring as per NIST-SI-04 by collecting essential information about RDP connections. Monitoring these logs (Event IDs 21, 24, 25 for TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager and 4624/4625 for Security log) helps detect unauthorized access attempts, including brute-force attacks (T1110.001), and suspicious activity on port 3389.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Restrict Inbound RDP
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc or manage via GPO).
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties.
3. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab.
4. Under 'State', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
5. Click 'Apply', then 'OK'.
6. In 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security', navigate to 'Inbound Rules'.
7. Create a 'New Rule...' to allow RDP (Port 3389) *only* from specific, trusted IP addresses or subnets:
   - Rule Type: 'Port'
   - Protocol and Ports: 'TCP', 'Specific local ports: 3389'
   - Action: 'Allow the connection'
   - Remote IP address: 'These IP addresses', then 'Add...' the trusted source IPs (e.g., your management subnet).
   - Profiles: Select 'Domain' (and other relevant profiles if needed).
   - Name: 'Allow RDP from Trusted Sources'
steps.explanation: This implements a network access control as per Windows Firewall control 9.1.2, significantly reducing the attack surface for RDP. By blocking all inbound connections by default and only allowing RDP from known, trusted sources, you limit the ability of unauthorized actors to attempt connections, including brute-force attacks (T1110.001).

steps.title: Disable Anonymous SAM Account and Share Enumeration
steps.command_or_action:
1. Open Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc or manage via GPO).
2. Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.
3. Double-click 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and and shares'.
4. Set the policy to 'Enabled'.
5. Click 'Apply', then 'OK'.
Alternatively, via Registry:
1. Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe).
2. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa.
3. Locate or create a DWORD (32-bit) Value named 'RestrictAnonymous'.
4. Set its 'Value data' to '1'.
steps.explanation: This hardening measure, as per Windows Server control 2.3.10.3, prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shares. This makes it significantly harder for attackers to gather valid usernames, which is a common precursor to brute-force attacks (T1110.001) against RDP or other services.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 machine(s)
Access to Group Policy Management Console (if managing via GPO)
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. After applying the Group Policy settings, run 'gpupdate /force' on the target system(s) and reboot if necessary.
2. Verify RDP logging: Check Event Viewer (Security log for Event IDs 4624/4625, and TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational for Event IDs 21, 24, 25) to confirm RDP connection events are being logged.
3. Verify Firewall rules: Open 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' and confirm that the 'Domain Profile' inbound connections are set to 'Block (default)' and that your specific RDP allow rule from trusted sources is active.
4. Verify SAM enumeration setting: Open Registry Editor (regedit.exe) and navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa. Confirm 'RestrictAnonymous' is set to '1'.

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). steps.title: Investigate Observed SSH Connection
steps.command_or_action: Review logs on 10.100.104.66 (destination) and any intermediate network devices (Palo Alto Firewall, Cisco Switch) for details regarding the SSH connection from 10.128.2.109. Determine if the connection was authorized, its purpose, and if it completed successfully.
steps.explanation: This initial investigation is critical to understand the nature of the observed connection. It helps differentiate between legitimate administrative access, a misconfiguration, or a potential security incident like unauthorized access or a brute-force attempt (T1110).

steps.title: Configure Permitted IP Addresses for SSH Management on Palo Alto Firewall
steps.command_or_action: 1. Navigate to Device > Setup > Management.2. Under 'Management Interface Settings', locate the 'Permitted IP Addresses' section for SSH.3. Add or modify the list to include only authorized IP addresses or networks that should be allowed to connect via SSH to the firewall's management interface.4. Commit the changes.
steps.explanation: Limiting SSH access to the firewall's management interface to only trusted IP addresses significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unauthorized access attempts and potential brute-force attacks (T1110) against the firewall itself. This aligns with Palo Alto Networks Firewall control 1.2.2.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Public Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: 1. Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' (wf.msc).2. In the left pane, click 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'.3. For the 'Public Profile' tab, ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'.4. Repeat for all relevant Windows Server and Windows 11 machines.
steps.explanation: Setting the Public profile to block inbound connections by default helps protect Windows machines from unsolicited connections, including potential SSH attempts, especially when they are exposed to less trusted networks. This aligns with Windows Firewall control CIS 9.3.2 / 9.3.2.

steps.title: Set NetBT NodeType Configuration on Windows Servers
steps.command_or_action: 1. Open the Registry Editor (regedit.exe).2. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters.3. Locate or create a DWORD (32-bit) Value named 'NodeType'.4. Set its value data to '2' (P-node).5. Restart the NetBIOS over TCP/IP service or the server for changes to take effect.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to P-node (Peer-to-Peer) helps reduce NetBIOS broadcast traffic and potential information leakage, contributing to a more secure network environment on Windows Servers. This addresses control 18.3.4 (L1).

steps.title: Implement or Verify SSH Monitoring on Ubuntu Linux Servers
steps.command_or_action: 1. Ensure auditd is installed and running: sudo apt update && sudo apt install auditd -y && sudo systemctl enable auditd --now.2. Add a rule to monitor SSH authentication attempts: sudo auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F path=/usr/bin/ssh -F key=ssh_auth_attempts and sudo auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -F path=/usr/bin/ssh -F key=ssh_auth_attempts.3. Add a rule to monitor SSH daemon activity: sudo auditctl -w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k ssh_log_monitor.4. Save the audit rules: sudo /sbin/augenrules --load.5. Configure log aggregation (e.g., to a SIEM) for /var/log/auth.log and audit.log for centralized analysis.
steps.explanation: Monitoring SSH activity, including successful and failed login attempts, is crucial for detecting unauthorized access, brute-force attacks (T1110), and other suspicious behavior. Centralized logging and analysis enable timely incident response. This aligns with NIST-SI-04.

steps.title: Implement or Verify SSH Monitoring on Windows Servers
steps.command_or_action: 1. If OpenSSH Server is installed, ensure logging is enabled. SSH server logs are typically found in C:\ProgramData\ssh\logs.2. Configure Windows Event Forwarding or a SIEM agent to collect logs from the following Event Logs: Security Log (Event ID 4624 for successful logins, 4625 for failed logins) and Microsoft-Windows-OpenSSH/Operational (if OpenSSH Server is used).3. Establish alerts for multiple failed SSH login attempts from a single source IP or to multiple accounts.
steps.explanation: Even if SSH is not the primary remote access method, monitoring its activity on Windows Servers is essential to detect unauthorized access attempts, including brute-force attacks (T1110), and ensure system integrity. This aligns with NIST-SI-04.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Palo Alto Firewall.
Administrative access to Windows Servers and Windows 11 machines.
Administrative access to Ubuntu Linux servers.
Familiarity with auditd on Linux.
Familiarity with Windows Registry Editor and Event Viewer.
Knowledge of legitimate SSH usage within the environment.
remediation.verification_procedure: Confirm that the Palo Alto Firewall's SSH management profile only permits authorized IP addresses. Verify that Windows Firewall Public profile inbound connections are set to 'Block (default)' on target machines. Confirm the 'NodeType' registry value is set to '2' on Windows Servers. Test SSH monitoring by attempting a failed login on an Ubuntu Linux server and verifying an alert is generated in the configured monitoring system. Test SSH monitoring by attempting a failed login on a Windows Server (if OpenSSH is enabled) and verifying an alert is generated. Review logs for 10.100.104.66 and network devices to confirm the nature of the original observed connection.

Boundary Protection

Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system; Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; and Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. steps.title: Restrict Permitted IP Addresses for SSH on Palo Alto Firewall Management Profiles
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to Device > Setup > Management. For each management profile where SSH is enabled, ensure the 'Permitted IP Addresses' list is configured to only allow known, authorized IP ranges or hosts.
steps.explanation: This prevents unauthorized access to the firewall's management interface via SSH, reducing the attack surface and mitigating potential brute force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server or Windows 11, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'. For the 'Public Profile', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'. Repeat for 'Domain Profile' and 'Private Profile' if applicable, ensuring a restrictive default.
steps.explanation: This ensures that all unsolicited inbound connections, including SSH, are blocked unless explicitly allowed by a specific firewall rule, thereby controlling network access (NIST-SC-07) and preventing unauthorized service discovery (T1046).

steps.title: Review and Refine Windows Firewall Rules for SSH
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server or Windows 11, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Review 'Inbound Rules'. Identify any rules allowing SSH (port 22). Ensure these rules are as restrictive as possible, specifying 'Remote IP address' to only authorized sources. Disable or delete any unnecessary SSH inbound rules.
steps.explanation: Even with a default block, specific rules might override it. This step ensures that only necessary and authorized SSH access is permitted to Windows machines, further enhancing boundary protection (NIST-SC-07).

steps.title: Verify and Enhance System Monitoring for SSH Connections
steps.command_or_action: Access the organization's SIEM or network monitoring solution. Confirm that logs from firewalls (Palo Alto) and relevant servers (Windows, Ubuntu) are being ingested and analyzed for SSH connection attempts. Ensure alerts are configured for: SSH connections from unknown or unauthorized source IPs; Excessive failed SSH login attempts (brute force indicators); SSH connections to sensitive systems.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring helps detect and respond to suspicious SSH activity, including unauthorized access attempts (T1110), potential data exfiltration over C2 channels (T1041), and unauthorized use of the system (NIST-SI-04).

prerequisites: Administrative access to Palo Alto Firewall.
Administrative access to Windows Server and Windows 11 machines.
Access to the organization's SIEM or network monitoring solution.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt to establish an SSH connection from an unauthorized IP address to a Palo Alto Firewall management interface. The connection should be blocked. 2. Attempt to establish an SSH connection from an unauthorized IP address to a Windows Server or Windows 11 machine. The connection should be blocked. 3. Verify that the SIEM or network monitoring solution generates alerts for any unauthorized SSH connection attempts or suspicious SSH activity.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. steps.title: Set Windows Firewall Domain Profile Inbound Connections to Block
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpmc.msc) or Local Security Policy (secpol.msc). Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Domain Profile' tab. Under 'State', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This ensures that all unsolicited inbound connections are blocked by default when the system is on a domain network, significantly reducing the attack surface, including for RDP. This directly addresses the potential for unauthorized RDP connections.

steps.title: Set Windows Firewall Public Profile Inbound Connections to Block
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpmc.msc) or Local Security Policy (secpol.msc). Navigate to Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties. Select the 'Public Profile' tab. Under 'State', set 'Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: This ensures that all unsolicited inbound connections are blocked by default when the system is on a public or untrusted network, providing a strong defense against external threats.

steps.title: Review and Restrict Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Inbound Rules
steps.command_or_action: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Inbound Rules'. Filter or search for rules related to 'Remote Desktop' or 'Port 3389'. For any existing RDP rules, right-click and select 'Properties'. Go to the 'Scope' tab and ensure 'Remote IP address' is set to 'These IP addresses' and specify only trusted source IP ranges (e.g., internal management subnets). Delete any overly permissive RDP rules (e.g., 'Any IP address').
steps.explanation: While the default inbound policy is set to block, explicit RDP rules might override this. Restricting RDP access to only necessary source IP addresses minimizes the risk of brute-force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized access.

steps.title: Configure Enhanced Monitoring for RDP Connections
steps.command_or_action: Ensure Windows Event Log forwarding is configured for Security Event ID 4624 (Successful Logon) and 4625 (Failed Logon) for RDP sessions (Logon Type 10). Configure your Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system to alert on multiple failed RDP logon attempts from a single source IP within a short timeframe, and on successful RDP logons from unusual source IPs or at unusual times.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring of RDP logon attempts helps detect and respond to brute-force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) and unauthorized access attempts in real-time, aligning with NIST-SI-04's objective of detecting attacks and unauthorized connections.

steps.title: Disable Network Security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.command_or_action: Open Group Policy Management Editor (gpmc.msc) or Local Security Policy (secpol.msc). Navigate to Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'. Set this policy to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This reduces the risk of information disclosure and unauthorized access through NULL sessions, enhancing overall system security.

prerequisites: Administrator privileges on the target Windows Server/11 systems.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (if managing via GPO).
Access to the SIEM system for configuring alerts.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Confirm Windows Firewall 'DefaultInboundAction' for Domain and Public profiles is 'Block' using PowerShell: Get-NetFirewallProfile -Name Domain | Select-Object Name, DefaultInboundAction; Get-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public | Select-Object Name, DefaultInboundAction. 2. Verify specific RDP inbound rules are restricted to trusted source IPs using PowerShell: Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Remote Desktop (TCP-In)" | Select-Object DisplayName, Enabled, Direction, Action, @{Name='RemoteAddresses';Expression={$_.RemoteAddress}}. 3. Confirm 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled' by navigating to the policy path in Local Security Policy or Group Policy. 4. Verify RDP logon events (Event IDs 4624, 4625) are being collected by the SIEM and that appropriate alerts are configured for suspicious activity.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows system (Windows Server or Windows 11), open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if managing via domain policy. Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as 'Everyone' for permissions, which reduces the attack surface and mitigates potential unauthorized access or information disclosure that could aid brute-force attacks (T1110).

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server system, open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' and set it to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this setting prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shared folders. This significantly hinders reconnaissance efforts (T1110.001) by attackers attempting to gather valid usernames or share information before launching brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: On the target Windows Server system, open the Local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) or Group Policy Management Console (GPMC). Navigate to: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options. Locate 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' and set it to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents the LocalSystem account from falling back to a NULL session when authenticating to network resources. This helps prevent potential unauthorized access or information disclosure through NULL sessions, further hardening the system against remote access attempts.

prerequisites: Administrator access to the target Windows Server or Windows 11 system.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if managing via domain policy.
remediation.verification_procedure:
After applying the Group Policy settings, force a Group Policy update by running 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated command prompt. Then, verify the settings by navigating to the UI Paths specified in the remediation steps and confirming they are set as prescribed. Alternatively, check the following registry keys:
- For Step 1: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous should be 0.
- For Step 2: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous should be 1.

steps.title: Configure Palo Alto Firewall for Port Scan Prevention
steps.command_or_action: Navigate to "Policies" > "Security" on the Palo Alto Firewall. Edit existing security policies or create new ones that allow traffic to or from the Internet. Under the "Actions" tab, ensure a "Vulnerability Protection" profile is applied that includes signatures for port scan detection and blocking. Additionally, review "DoS Protection" profiles under "Objects" > "Security Profiles" > "DoS Protection" and apply them to relevant zones/interfaces to detect and block flood-based scanning.
steps.explanation: Implementing robust vulnerability and DoS protection profiles on the Palo Alto Firewall helps detect and automatically block aggressive port scanning attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1041) before they reach internal hosts, preventing service discovery and potential exploitation. This aligns with Palo Alto Networks Firewall control 6.14 by applying a secure profile to internet-facing policies.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Public Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server (10.128.1.117), open "Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security". Navigate to "Windows Defender Firewall Properties". For the "Public Profile" tab, ensure "Inbound connections" is set to "Block (default)". Apply this setting.
steps.explanation: Setting the Windows Firewall's Public Profile to block inbound connections prevents unsolicited network traffic from reaching the server, significantly reducing the attack surface and mitigating port scanning (MITRE ATT&CK T1041) attempts by making services undiscoverable from external networks. This directly implements Windows Firewall control 9.3.2 / CIS 9.3.2.

steps.title: Set NetBT NodeType to P-node on Windows Server
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows Server (10.128.1.117), open the Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters. Create or modify the DWORD (32-bit) Value named NodeType. Set its value data to 2 (P-node). Restart the server for the change to take effect.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) configures the system to use a WINS server for NetBIOS name resolution, reducing reliance on broadcast-based name resolution. This minimizes network broadcast traffic and can make the host slightly less discoverable via NetBIOS enumeration, contributing to overall system hardening as per Windows Server control 18.3.4.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Palo Alto Firewall.
Administrative access to the Windows Server 10.128.1.117.
remediation.verification_procedure: From an external host, attempt a port scan against 10.128.1.117. Verify that the Palo Alto Firewall logs show blocked attempts and that the Windows Server does not respond to unsolicited inbound connections. On the Windows Server, verify the NodeType registry value is 2.

steps.title: Block the malicious IP at the network perimeter
steps.command_or_action: On the Palo Alto Firewall, navigate to 'Policies' -> 'Security'. Create a new security rule with the following parameters: Action: Deny, Source Zone: (External/Untrust Zone), Source Address: 91.189.91.39, Destination Zone: (Internal/Trust Zone), Destination Address: 192.168.220.73, Application: any, Service/URL Category: any. Position this rule at the top of your security policy ruleset to ensure it takes precedence. Commit the changes.
steps.explanation: This step immediately stops the high volume of connections from the identified malicious source, preventing further denial-of-service attempts (T1110) and potential data exfiltration (T1041, T1010.003, T1573.002) or reconnaissance (T1518.001). This action aligns with NIST-SC-07 Boundary Protection by controlling communications at external managed interfaces.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall on the target server
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server 192.168.220.73, open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security'. Navigate to 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties' -> 'Public Profile' tab. Ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Alternatively, create a specific inbound rule under 'Inbound Rules' to block all traffic from the remote IP address 91.189.91.39 to any port on 192.168.220.73.
steps.explanation: This provides host-level protection, ensuring that the server itself blocks unsolicited inbound connections. This helps mitigate denial-of-service attempts (T1110) and prevents unauthorized access that could lead to data exfiltration (T1041, T1010.003, T1573.002) or further compromise using native APIs (T1106, T1106.001) or shared modules (T1129). This aligns with Windows 9.3.2 and CIS 9.3.2.

steps.title: Ensure NetBT NodeType configuration is set to P-node
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server 192.168.220.73, open Registry Editor (regedit.exe). Navigate to 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters'. Locate or create a DWORD (32-bit) Value named 'NodeType'. Set its value data to '2' (for P-node). Restart the NetBT service or the server for the change to take effect.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT to P-node (peer-to-peer) reduces reliance on broadcast-based name resolution, which can be susceptible to spoofing and enumeration. While not directly addressing the high connection volume, it improves overall network security posture and reduces the attack surface for discovery (T1518.001) and potential lateral movement. This aligns with Windows Server 18.3.4.

steps.title: Enhance network monitoring and alerting for suspicious activity
steps.command_or_action: Review and configure network monitoring tools (e.g., Palo Alto Firewall logs, network flow data) to generate alerts for: unusual connection volumes to/from internal hosts, connections from known malicious IPs or to suspicious external IPs, and outbound connections from internal servers to non-standard ports or protocols. Analyze existing logs for 91.189.91.39 to identify the specific service/port targeted on 192.168.220.73.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring is crucial for detecting ongoing or future attacks, including denial-of-service attempts (T1110), data exfiltration over C2 channels (T1041, T1010.003, T1573.002), and reconnaissance (T1518.001). This aligns with NIST-SI-04 System Monitoring and NIST-SC-07 Boundary Protection by monitoring and controlling communications and analyzing detected events and anomalies.

steps.title: Investigate the target host for compromise
steps.command_or_action: On Windows Server 192.168.220.73: Examine system logs (Event Viewer: Security, System, Application) for suspicious activity around the time of the high connection volume. Check running processes and services for unknown or unauthorized entries. Scan the system with up-to-date antivirus/EDR software. Review scheduled tasks and startup programs. Analyze network connections from the host using 'netstat -ano' to identify processes making connections.
steps.explanation: A high volume of connections, especially if inbound, could indicate a successful compromise or an attempt to exploit a vulnerability. Investigating the host helps identify if an attacker has gained initial access (T1110), performed software discovery (T1518.001), or is using native APIs (T1106, T1106.001), shared modules (T1129), or trusted developer utilities (T1127) to maintain persistence, escalate privileges, or prepare for data exfiltration (T1041, T1010.003, T1573.002). This aligns with NIST-SI-04 System Monitoring by analyzing detected events and anomalies.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Palo Alto Firewall.
Administrative access to the Windows Server 192.168.220.73.
Access to network monitoring tools and logs.
remediation.verification_procedure: Confirm the malicious IP 91.189.91.39 is blocked on the Palo Alto Firewall. Verify Windows Firewall settings on 192.168.220.73 ensure inbound connections are blocked or a specific deny rule for 91.189.91.39 is active. Confirm NetBT NodeType is set to P-node (value 2) in the registry. Monitor network traffic to ensure no further connections from 91.189.91.39 are observed. Review host logs and scan results on 192.168.220.73 for any signs of compromise or suspicious activity.

Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary). communications at external managed interfaces to the system are monitored; communications at external managed interfaces to the system are controlled; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are monitored; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are controlled; subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; external networks or systems are only connected to through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. System and communications protection policy procedures addressing boundary protection list of key internal boundaries of the system system design documentation boundary protection hardware and software system configuration settings and associated documentation enterprise security architecture documentation system audit records system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities system developer organizational personnel with boundary protection responsibilities Mechanisms implementing boundary protection capabilities

System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. steps.title: Block suspicious outbound traffic at the perimeter firewall
steps.command_or_action:
On the Palo Alto Firewall, navigate to Policies > Security. Click 'Add' to create a new security policy with the following parameters:
Source Zone: [Identify the internal zone where 192.168.220.73 resides, e.g., 'Trust']
Destination Zone: [Identify the external/untrust zone, e.g., 'Untrust']
Source Address: 192.168.220.73
Destination Address: 91.189.91.39
Application: any
Service: any
Action: Deny
Log at Session End: Yes
Click 'OK' and then 'Commit' the changes to apply the policy.
steps.explanation: This step immediately blocks the high volume of connections from the internal source IP to the external destination IP, preventing further potential denial-of-service attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1499) or data exfiltration. This aligns with NIST-SC-07 for boundary protection and controlling communications at external interfaces.

steps.title: Isolate and investigate the source internal IP address
steps.command_or_action:
1. Identify the device associated with IP 192.168.220.73 using network tools (e.g., ARP tables on Cisco Switches, DHCP server logs, or network access control systems).
2. Once identified, disconnect the device from the network to prevent further malicious activity.
3. Perform a thorough malware scan and forensic analysis on the identified device to determine the root cause of the suspicious connections (e.g., malware infection, compromised credentials, misconfiguration).
steps.explanation: Identifying and isolating the source device is critical to understand the nature of the activity (e.g., a compromised host attempting DoS - MITRE ATT&CK T1499, or data exfiltration) and prevent recurrence. This step aligns with NIST-SI-04 for analyzing detected events and identifying unauthorized use.
steps.vendor_product: Windows Server, Windows 11, Ubuntu Linux, Cisco Switch

steps.title: Ensure Windows Firewall inbound connections are blocked by default
steps.command_or_action:
For Windows Server and Windows 11 machines, open PowerShell as an administrator and execute the following command to ensure the Public profile is enabled and inbound connections are blocked by default:
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -Enabled True -DefaultInboundAction Block
steps.explanation: This step hardens Windows systems by ensuring that unsolicited inbound connections on the Public network profile are blocked by default, reducing the attack surface. This directly addresses controls 9.3.2 and CIS 9.3.2.

steps.title: Review and enhance network monitoring and alerting for unusual traffic patterns
steps.command_or_action:
1. Review existing SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) or Palo Alto Firewall logs for any alerts related to high connection volumes, unusual outbound destinations, or other indicators of compromise.
2. Configure or refine alerts in your Palo Alto Firewall and/or SIEM to detect:
   - High volume of outbound connections from internal subnets to external IPs.
   - Connections to known malicious IPs (if threat intelligence feeds are integrated).
   - Unusual protocols or ports used for outbound traffic from internal hosts.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring and alerting are essential for early detection of potential denial-of-service attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1499) or data exfiltration activities. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring to detect attacks and unauthorized connections.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Palo Alto Firewall.
Network diagram and IP address management (IPAM) system to identify the device at 192.168.220.73.
Administrative access to the device at 192.168.220.73 (if identified).
Administrative access to Windows Servers and Windows 11 machines.
Access to network monitoring tools and/or SIEM.
remediation.verification_procedure:
1. Confirm that no further connections from 192.168.220.73 to 91.189.91.39 are observed in Palo Alto Firewall logs after applying the blocking rule.
2. Verify that the device at 192.168.220.73 has been isolated, investigated, and remediated (if compromised).
3. Confirm that Windows Firewall 'Public: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)' on relevant Windows systems.
4. Validate that new or enhanced monitoring alerts are configured and functioning correctly.

steps.title: Block outbound traffic to suspicious IP at perimeter firewall
steps.command_or_action: 1. Log in to the Palo Alto Firewall administrative interface.2. Navigate to Policies > Security.3. Click 'Add' to create a new security rule.4. Configure the rule with the following parameters:   - Name: Block_Outbound_Malicious_IP_91.189.91.39   - Source Zone: [Identify the appropriate internal zone, e.g., 'Trust']   - Source Address: 10.100.105.2   - Destination Zone: [Identify the appropriate external zone, e.g., 'Untrust']   - Destination Address: 91.189.91.39   - Application: any   - Service/URL Category: any   - Action: Deny   - Log at Session End: Yes5. Position the rule above any 'allow' rules that might permit this traffic.6. Commit the changes to the firewall.
steps.explanation: This step immediately stops the high volume of outbound connections from the internal host (10.100.105.2) to the suspicious external IP (91.189.91.39) at the network boundary. This mitigates potential data exfiltration, command and control communications (T1016.001), or the internal host participating in a denial-of-service attack (T1498) as per NIST-SC-07 boundary protection principles.

steps.title: Configure host-based firewall to block outbound connections (Windows)
steps.command_or_action: On the Windows machine (10.100.105.2), open an elevated PowerShell prompt and execute the following command:New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Outbound to 91.189.91.39" -Direction Outbound -Action Block -RemoteAddress 91.189.91.39 -Protocol Any
steps.explanation: This provides an additional layer of defense at the host level, preventing the potentially compromised or misconfigured Windows host from initiating connections to the suspicious external IP. This reinforces the network boundary protection and directly mitigates the host's ability to perform network connection discovery (T1016.001) or participate in a network DoS (T1498). This aligns with Windows Firewall controls (9.3.2, CIS 9.3.2, 9.1.2).

steps.title: Configure host-based firewall to block outbound connections (Ubuntu Linux)
steps.command_or_action: On the Ubuntu Linux machine (10.100.105.2), open a terminal and execute the following commands:sudo ufw insert 1 deny out to 91.189.91.39sudo ufw enable
steps.explanation: Similar to Windows Firewall, this provides host-level protection, preventing the potentially compromised or misconfigured Ubuntu host from initiating connections to the suspicious external IP. This reinforces network boundary protection and directly mitigates the host's ability to perform network connection discovery (T1016.001) or participate in a network DoS (T1498).

steps.title: Investigate the internal host for compromise or misconfiguration
steps.command_or_action: Perform a comprehensive investigation on the host 10.100.105.2. This includes:1. Running a full antivirus/anti-malware scan.2. Reviewing system logs (e.g., Windows Event Logs, syslog on Ubuntu) for suspicious activity, errors, or unauthorized access.3. Checking running processes and services for unknown or malicious entries.4. Analyzing network connections from the host to identify the specific process or application generating the high volume traffic.5. Reviewing scheduled tasks and startup items.
steps.explanation: Blocking traffic is a containment measure. This step focuses on identifying and remediating the root cause of the high volume outbound connections, which could be a compromise (e.g., malware, botnet membership) or a misconfiguration. Addressing the root cause is crucial to prevent recurrence and fully mitigate the threat associated with T1016.001 (discovery) and T1498 (DoS participation).

prerequisites: Administrative access to Palo Alto Firewall.
Administrative access to the internal host 10.100.105.2 (Windows Server/11 or Ubuntu Linux).
Network diagrams or knowledge of network zones to correctly configure firewall rules.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Monitor Palo Alto Firewall logs for denied connections from 10.100.105.2 to 91.189.91.39, confirming the rule is active and blocking traffic.2. Confirm that the original alert regarding high volume connections has ceased.3. From the internal host 10.100.105.2, attempt to ping or establish a connection to 91.189.91.39 and verify that it fails, indicating host-based firewall rules are effective.
